¶702.content: - 3.6.4.2.1 Distance information received from trackside shall be evaluated on-board as nominal information (without taking into account any tolerances).

¶703.content: - 3.6.4.2.2 Note: The confidence interval increases in relation to the distance travelled from the LRBG depending on the accuracy of odometer equipment until it is reset when another balise group becomes the LRBG.

¶704.content: - 3.6.4.2.3 The  value  of  the  Location  Accuracy  shall  be  determined  by  Linking  information  if available,  if  not,  by  the  corresponding  National  Value,  or  the  corresponding  Default Value if the National Value is not applicable.

¶705.content: - 3.6.4.3 When another balise group becomes the LRBG or when evaluating (see section 4.8) location related trackside information, which is referred to a previously received balise group different from the LRBG, all the location related information shall be relocated by subtracting from the distances that are counted from the reference balise group of the location related information:

¶706.content: - the distance between the reference balise group of the location related information and the LRBG, retrieved from linking information if it is available and it includes both the reference balise group and the LRBG, OR

¶707.content: - in  all  other  cases,  the  estimated  travelled  distance  between  the  reference  balise group of the location related information and the LRBG.

¶708.content: - 3.6.4.3.1 Justification:  it  is  always  the  trackside  responsibility  to  provide  linking  in  due  course, knowing this rule; if the location related information is to be used in situations where linking is not provided (e.g. TSR transmitted by balise group marked as unlinked), the trackside can include provisions, if deemed necessary, when engineering the distance information.

¶709.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶711.content: When the ERTMS/ETCS on-board has read the balise group 1

¶712.content: -  the Confidence Interval is reset taking into account the location accuracy of balise group 1, and on-board tolerances when determining the reference location of the balise group,

¶713.content: -  the nominal distance to EOA is relocated by subtracting D_LINK (1) from nominal distance (1), resulting in nominal distance (2). (Nominal distance (1) may be the distance to EOA received in the MA or the result of a previous relocation.)

¶714.content: Figure 13a: Reset of confidence interval and relocation, on change of LRBG

¶715.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶716.content: When the on-board performs the transition to level 2, the MA stored on-board (referred to balise group 0) is relocated prior to its evaluation:

¶717.content: -  If balise group 1 is still the LRBG, the first MA Section distance is relocated by subtracting D_LINK (1) from MA Section (1), resulting in MA section (1')

¶718.content: -  If balise group 2 is already the LRBG, the first MA Section distance is relocated by subtracting (D_LINK (1) + D_LINK (2)) from MA Section (1), resulting in MA section (1')

¶720.content: # Figure  13b:  Relocation  of  trackside  information  referred  to  previously  passed balise group, different from the current LRBG

¶721.content: - 3.6.4.4 The train front end position shall be identified in the following way

¶723.content: - The max(imum) safe front end position, differing from the estimated position by the under-reading amount in the distance measured from the LRBG plus the location accuracy of the LRBG.

¶724.content: - I.e. in relation to the orientation of the train this position is in advance of the estimated position.

¶725.content: -  The min(imum) safe front end position, differing from the estimated position by the over-reading amount in the distance measured from the LRBG plus the location accuracy of the LRBG.

¶726.content: - I.e. in relation to the orientation of the train this position is in rear of the estimated position.

¶727.content: - 3.6.4.4.1 Note: The rear end position is referenced in the same way. However min safe rear end is only safe if sent together with train integrity information.

¶728.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶729.content: Figure 13c: Train confidence interval and train front end position

¶731.content: - 3.6.4.6 The estimated front end shall be used when supervising location information, unless stated otherwise.

¶732.content: - 3.6.4.7 Supervision  of  location  related  information  transmitted  by  a  balise  group  marked  as unlinked and referred to this balise group:

¶733.content: - 3.6.4.7.1 By exception to clause 3.6.1.3, the train position is referred to this balise group marked as  unlinked.  The  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  temporarily  apply  by analogy  clauses  3.6.4.2,  3.6.4.2.1,  3.6.4.2.3  and  3.6.4.4  to  determine  an  additional confidence  interval,  until  a  further  received  balise  group  becomes  the  LRBG  or  the location related information is deleted on-board.

¶734.content: - 3.6.4.7.2 If another balise group marked as unlinked is received before the additional confidence interval is deleted:

¶735.content: - the additional confidence interval shall be reset in relation to this new balise group marked as unlinked

¶736.content: - the  location  related  information  referred  to  the  previous  balise  group  marked  as unlinked shall be relocated by subtracting the estimated travelled distance between both  balise  groups  from  the  distances  that  are  counted  from  this  previous  balise group marked as unlinked.

¶739.content: - 3.6.5.1.1 The position shall refer to the front end of the respective engine with regards to the train orientation.

¶740.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶742.content: - 3.6.5.1.2 The position report shall contain at least the following position and direction data

¶743.content: - The distance between the LRBG and the estimated front end of the train.

¶744.content: - The distance from the estimated front end position to the min safe front end position and the distance from the estimated front end position to the max safe front end position.

¶745.content: -  The identity of the location reference, the LRBG.

¶746.content: - The orientation of the train in relation to the LRBG orientation. Note: Driver selected running direction is only handled by the on-board system.

¶747.content: - The position of the front end of the train in relation to the LRBG (nominal or reverse side of the LRBG).

¶750.content: - Direction of train movement in relation to the LRBG orientation

¶752.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶756.content: Train orientation in relation to LRBG

¶762.content: front end to max safe front end

¶763.content: # Figure  14:  Information  given  in  a  position  report  (two  examples  to  show  the relation between LRBG and train orientation)

¶765.content: - 3.6.5.1.4 The on-board equipment shall send position reports as requested by the RBC in the position report parameters. In addition, it shall also send a position report if at least one of the events listed hereafter occurs:

¶766.content: - The train reaches standstill, if applicable to the current mode.

¶769.content: - A loss of train integrity is detected.

¶770.content: - The train passes a RBC/RBC border with its min safe rear end.

¶771.content: -  The train passes with its min safe rear end a level transition border which led to a transition from level 2/3 to level 0, NTC or 1.

¶774.content: - A communication session is successfully established.

¶776.content: - The  train  passes  an  LRBG  compliant  balise  group  (see  3.6.2.2.2),  if  no  position report parameters are stored on-board.

¶777.content: -  The train passes a RBC/RBC border with its max safe front end.

¶778.content: - An error as defined in 3.16.4 is detected.

¶779.content: - 3.6.5.1.4.1 If the position report results from one or more events listed in 3.6.5.1.4, its content shall reflect the consequences of these events.

¶780.content: - 3.6.5.1.5 For  the  position  report  parameters  requested  by  the  RBC  the  following  possibilities shall be available, individually or in combination

¶783.content: -  When  the  max  safe  front  end  or  min  safe  rear  end  of  the  train  has  passed  a specified location.

¶784.content: - At every passage of an LRBG compliant balise group (see 3.6.2.2.2).

¶786.content: - 3.6.5.1.5.1 Note: d) and e) can not be combined.

¶788.content: - 3.6.5.1.7 The  given  position  report  parameters  shall  be  valid  until  new  parameters  are  given from the RBC.

¶789.content: - 3.6.5.1.8 The mode and level reported in a position report shall be consistent (e.g., no mode that relates to the previous level).

¶790.content: # 3.6.5.2 Report of Train Rear End Position for Level 3

¶791.content: - 3.6.5.2.1 Train integrity information shall be given by external device or by driver.

¶792.content: - 3.6.5.2.2 Driver input of train integrity shall only be permitted at standstill.

¶793.content: - 3.6.5.2.3 The train integrity information shall consist of

¶796.content: -  Train integrity information confirmed by integrity monitoring device

¶797.content: -  Train integrity information confirmed (entered) by driver

¶799.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶800.content: - Safe train length information (only valid if train integrity is confirmed at the same time).

¶801.content: - 3.6.5.2.4 The safe train  length  information  shall  represent  the  distance  between  the  min  safe rear end (by subtracting the train length from the min. safe front end position at the time when integrity was established last time) and the estimated position of the train front.

¶802.content: - 3.6.5.2.5 The safe train length information shall be re-calculated for every position report using the same last value of min safe rear end position until a new min safe rear end position is established on-board taking into account the time to detect train integrity.

¶803.content: Figure 15: Calculation of Safe Train Length when train integrity was established

¶805.content: - 3.6.6.1 The  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  display,  only  on  driver  request,  the geographical  position  of  the  estimated  front  end  of  the  train  in  relation  to  the  track kilometre.  The  display  of  the  geographical  position  shall  also  be  stopped  on  driver request.

¶806.content: - 3.6.6.2 The resolution of the position indication shall be 1 metre (sufficient to allow the driver to report the train position when communicating with the signalman).

¶807.content: - 3.6.6.3 When  receiving  new  geographical  position  information  (from  radio  or  from  balise group),  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  replace  the  currently  stored

¶808.content: geographical  position  information  (if  any)  by  this  new  received  one,  continuing  the ongoing geographical position calculation until at least one of the condition of 3.6.6.9 applies.

¶809.content: - 3.6.6.4 Geographical  position  information  shall  always  use  a  balise  group  as  geographical position  reference  balise  group  and  if  needed  an  offset  from  that  balise  group.  A geographical position reference balise group shall be either:

¶810.content: - part of the last reported balise groups memorised on-board, in case the information is transmitted by radio, OR

¶811.content: - the balise group transmitting the information, in case the information is transmitted by balise group, OR

¶812.content: -  any balise group not yet passed at the time of reception of the information.

¶813.content: - 3.6.6.4.1 In  case  the  information  is  received  by  radio  and  at  least  one  of  the  announced geographical  position  reference  balise  group(s)  is  part  of  the  last  reported  balise groups memorised on-board, the on-board equipment shall use the data related to the most recently reported balise group.

¶814.content: - 3.6.6.4.2 From  the  currently  stored  geographical  position  information,  the  track  kilometre reference  given  for  a  geographical  reference  location  shall  become  applicable  if  the train  has  detected  the  related  geographical reference balise group and has travelled the offset distance from this reference balise group.

¶815.content: - 3.6.6.4.3 The announced and not applicable geographical references shall be deleted on-board if the train changes orientation.

¶816.content: - 3.6.6.5 The  distance  travelled  from  the  geographical  reference  location  shall  be  taken  into account when calculating the geographical position.

¶817.content: - 3.6.6.6 In  cases  where  the  track  kilometre  is  not  incremental  (jumps,  changes  in  counting direction,  scaling  error)  the  reported  position  might  be  wrong  between  the  point  of irregularity and the next new reference.

¶818.content: - 3.6.6.7 In cases where single balise groups are used as a reference for geographical position information and where no linking information is available (and therefore no orientation can  be  assigned  to  the  balise  group),  the  on-board  equipment  shall  ignore  the geographical position information related to these single balise groups.

¶820.content: - 3.6.6.9 The on-board equipment shall continue calculating the position from a track kilometre reference (i.e. this track kilometre reference shall remain applicable) until:

¶821.content: - a new track kilometre reference becomes applicable, OR

¶822.content: - it is told not to do so, OR

¶823.content: -  the calculated geographical position becomes negative, OR

¶824.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶825.content: - no  more  geographical  position  information  is  available  (e.g.,  deleted  according  to conditions in SRS chapter 4)

¶826.content: - 3.6.6.9.1 Once a track kilometre reference is no longer applicable, it shall be deleted.

¶827.content: - 3.6.6.10 The following data shall be included in a message for geographical position (for every track kilometre reference):

¶828.content: -  Identity of the geographical position reference balise group

¶829.content: -  Distance from geographical position reference balise group to the track kilometre reference (offset)

¶830.content: -  Value of the track kilometre reference

¶831.content: -  Counting  direction  of  the  track  kilometre  in  relation  to  the  geographical  position reference balise group orientation.

¶833.content: # 3.7 Completeness of data for safe train movement

¶835.content: - 3.7.1.1 To control the train movement in an ERTMS/ETCS based system the ERTMS/ETCS on-board  equipment  shall  be  given  information  from  the  trackside  system  both concerning  the  route  set  for  the  train  and  the  track  description  for  that  route.  The following information shall be given from the trackside

¶836.content: - Permission and distance to run, the Movement Authority (MA) (see section 3.8)

¶837.content: - When needed, limitations related to the movement authority, i.e. Mode profile for On Sight, Limited Supervision or Shunting and signalling related speed restriction (see

¶838.content: sections  3.12.4  and  3.11.6).  Mode  profile  and  Signalling  related  Speed  restriction shall always be sent together with the MA to which the information belongs

¶839.content: -  Track  description  covering  as  a  minimum  the  whole  distance  defined  by  the  MA. Track description includes the following information

¶842.content: -  Optionally Axle load Speed Profile (ASP) (see section 3.11.4)

¶843.content: -  Optionally  Speed  restriction  to  ensure  a  given  permitted  braking  distance  (see section 3.11.11)

¶846.content: -  Optionally areas where reversing is permitted (see section 3.15.4).

¶850.content: - 3.7.2.1 The Movement Authority (MA) shall be given to the on-board equipment

¶851.content: -  Together with the other information ( as listed in section 3.7.1.1 c) and d)) or

¶852.content: -  Separately, if the other information has already been correctly received by the onboard equipment.

¶853.content: - 3.7.2.2 The trackside shall be responsible for that the on-board equipment has received the information valid for the distance covered by the Movement Authority.

¶854.content: - 3.7.2.2.1 In  case  of  LOA,  trackside  shall  be  responsible  for  including  any  track  description beyond the LOA relevant for calculating safe supervision limits.

¶855.content: - 3.7.2.3 The MA and the related mode profile, if any, shall not be accepted by the on-board equipment if the SSP and gradient already available on-board or given together with the MA do not cover the full length of the MA.

¶856.content: - 3.7.2.3.1 Full length means at least from the estimated front end of the train to the supervised location.

¶857.content: - 3.7.2.4 It shall be possible for the trackside to send additional information when needed. The information referred to is

¶859.content: -  Request to stop earlier (from RBC only)

¶862.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶865.content: -  Inhibition of revocable TSRs from balises in L2/3 (from RBC only)

¶867.content: # 3.7.3 Extension, replacement of track description and linking information

¶868.content: - 3.7.3.1 New track description and linking information shall replace (in the ERTMS/ETCS onboard equipment) stored information as detailed below:

¶869.content: - New Static  Speed  Profile  information  shall  replace  all  stored  Static  Speed  Profile information from the start location of the new information.

¶870.content: - New Gradient Profile information shall replace all stored Gradient Profile information from the start location of the new information.

¶871.content: -  New Axle Load Speed Profile information shall replace all stored Axle Load Speed Profile information from the start location of the first element of the new information.

¶872.content: - New  Speed  Restriction  to  ensure  Permitted  Braking  Distance  information  shall replace all stored Speed  Restriction to ensure Permitted Braking Distance information from the start location of the first element of the new information.

¶873.content: - New track condition Change of Traction System information shall replace all stored Change of Traction System information.

¶874.content: -  New track condition Big Metal Masses information shall replace all stored Big Metal Masses  information  from  the  start  location  of the first  element  of  the  new information.

¶875.content: - New track condition information of at least one of the types listed here, i.e., sound horn, non stopping area, tunnel stopping area, powerless section -lower pantograph,  powerless  section  -  switch  off  the  main  power  switch,  radio  hole,  air tightness,  switch  off  regenerative  brake,  switch  off  eddy  current  brake  for  service brake, switch off eddy current brake for emergency brake, switch off magnetic shoe brake, shall replace all stored track condition information of the listed types from the start location of the first element of the new information.

¶876.content: - New  route  suitability  loading  gauge  information  shall  replace  all  stored  route suitability loading gauge information.

¶877.content: - New  route  suitability  traction  system  information  shall  replace  all  stored  route suitability traction system information.

¶878.content: - New route suitability  axle  load  information  shall  replace  all  stored  route  suitability axle load information.

¶879.content: -  New reversing area information shall replace all stored reversing area information.

¶880.content: - New adhesion factor information shall replace all stored adhesion factor information from the start location of the new information.

¶881.content: -  New  linking  information  received  as  non-infill  information  shall  replace  all  stored linking information from the LRBG.

¶882.content: - New linking information received as infill information shall replace all stored linking information from the reference location of the infill information (i.e. the balise group at next main signal).

¶883.content: - New  track  condition  Station  Platform  information  shall  replace  all  stored  Station Platform  information  from  the  start  location  of  the  first  element  of  the  new information.

¶884.content: - New  track  condition  Allowed  Current  Consumption  information  shall  replace  all stored Allowed Current Consumption information.

¶887.content: - 3.7.3.2 When requested  by  trackside,  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  resume initial states beyond a given location individually:

¶888.content: - for stored Speed Restriction to ensure Permitted Braking Distance information (for initial state, refer to 3.11.11.11)

¶889.content: - for stored axle load speed profile information (for initial state, refer to 3.11.4.5)

¶890.content: -  through a single request, for all stored track condition information of the following types:  sound  horn,  non  stopping  area,  tunnel  stopping  area,  powerless  section  lower pantograph, powerless section - switch off the main power switch, radio hole, air tightness, switch off regenerative brake, switch off eddy current brake for service brake, switch off eddy current brake for emergency brake and switch off magnetic shoe brake (for initial states, refer to 3.12.1.3)

¶891.content: - through a single request, for all stored route suitability information (for initial state, refer to 3.12.2.10).

¶892.content: - through a single request, for all stored track condition information of the type Station Platform (for initial state, refer to 3.12.1.3).

¶893.content: - 3.7.3.3 In  some  situations,  the  track  description  and  linking  information  shall  be  deleted  (or initial state shall be resumed) by the on-board equipment. These various cases where the  data  is  affected  (e.g.  the  MA  is  shortened)  are  described  in  detail  in  Appendix A.3.4.

¶896.content: - 3.7.3.6 Note: regarding the handling of Temporary Speed Restrictions and Level Crossings, see sections 3.11.5 and 3.12.5.

¶899.content: - 3.8.1.1 The following characteristics can be used in a Movement Authority (see Figure 17: Structure of an MA):

¶900.content: - The End of Movement Authority is the location to which the train is authorised to move.

¶901.content: - When  the  Target  Speed  at  the  End  of  Movement  Authority  is  zero,  the  End  of Movement Authority is called EOA (End of Authority); when the target speed is not zero, it is called the LOA (Limit of Authority). This non zero target speed can be time limited.

¶902.content: -  If  no  overlap  exists,  the  Danger  Point  is  a  location  beyond  the  End  of  Movement Authority  that  can  be  reached  by  the  front  end  of  the  train  without  a  risk  for  a hazardous situation.

¶903.content: - The  end  of  an  overlap  (if  used  in  the  existing  interlocking  system)  is  a  location beyond the Danger Point that can be reached by the front end of the train without a risk  for  a  hazardous  situation.  This  additional  distance  is  only  valid  for  a  defined time.

¶904.content: - A release speed is a speed limit under which the train is allowed to run in the vicinity of  the  End  of  Movement  Authority,  when  the  target  speed  is  zero.  One  release speed can be associated with the Danger Point, and another one with the overlap. Release speed can also be calculated on-board the train (see section 3.13.9.4).

¶905.content: -  The MA can be split into several sections, The last one is called End Section.

¶906.content: -  A first time-out value can be attached to each section. This value will be used for the revocation of the associated route when the train has not entered into it yet. It is called the Section time-out.

¶907.content: -  In addition, a second time-out value can be attached to the End Section of the MA. This second time-out will be used for the revocation of the last section when it is occupied by the train; it is called the End Section time-out.

¶908.content: - 3.8.1.2 The values of the time-outs possibly given in an MA shall take into account the time elapsed from the start of validity of information to the sending of the message.

¶909.content: - 3.8.1.3 Note: A Danger Point can be (not exhaustive list):

¶910.content: -  the  entry  point  of  an  occupied  block  section  (if  the  line  is  operated  according  to fixed block principles)

¶911.content: -  the  position  of  the  safe  rear  end  of  a  train  (if  the  line  is  operated  according  to moving block principles)

¶912.content: -  the  fouling  point  of  a  switch,  positioned  for  a  route,  conflicting  with  the  current direction  of  movement  of  the  train  (both  for  fixed  and  moving  block  mode  of operation)

¶913.content: - 3.8.1.4 Note: Traditionally the overlap is a piece of track (beyond the danger point), that is put at disposal of a train, to guarantee a non hazardous situation, also in case the driver should misjudge the stopping distance for the train. In ERTMS/ETCS the overlap can be used to improve the efficiency of the braking supervision.

¶914.content: - 3.8.1.5 Note:  Time-out  values  can  be  given  in  the  MA  to  cope  with  the  following  situations depending on the interlocking operations, i.e. the timers on-board will only reflect the situation trackside and when expired (on-board) the actions taken are restrictive:

¶915.content: - Section time-out or time-out for the speed at the LOA: When a signalman requests a route release of a part of a route not yet entered by the approaching train.

¶916.content: - End  Section  time-out:  When  the  train  has  entered  the  last  part  of  a  route,  the automatic route release can be delayed to make sure that the train has come to a standstill before any switches inside the route can be moved.

¶917.content: -  Time-out  for  an  overlap:  When  the  train  has  entered  the  last  part  of  a  route,  the overlap associated with the route remains valid for a certain time to make sure that the  train  has  successfully  stopped  before  its  End  of  Movement  Authority.  If  the overlap  is  still  unoccupied  when  the  timer  expires  the  interlocking  revokes  the overlap.

¶918.content: - 3.8.1.6 Note: If the trackside equipment does not have enough information to give the distance to  the  End  of  Movement Authority with a target speed equal to zero, a target speed higher than zero can be given (LOA, Limit of Authority). It is the responsibility of the trackside to ensure that the safe distance beyond the LOA is long enough to brake the train  from  the  target  speed  to  a  stand  still  without  any  hazardous  situation.  It  is  the responsibility of the on-board equipment to apply the brakes if no new information is received when the Limit of Authority is passed.

¶920.content: - 3.8.2.1 It shall be possible for the on-board equipment to request a new Movement Authority from the RBC.

¶921.content: - 3.8.2.2 The parameters for requesting a new MA shall be given by the RBC.

¶922.content: - 3.8.2.3 In level 2/3, the following possibilities shall be available:

¶923.content: - A  defined  time  before  the  train  reaches  the  perturbation  location  assuming  it  is running at the warning speed (see section 3.13.11 for details).

¶924.content: - A defined time before the Section timer (not the End Section timer, not the Overlap timer) for any section of the MA expires, or before the LOA speed timer expires.

¶925.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶926.content: - 3.8.2.3.1 Regards to the above possibilities, the MA request shall be triggered when the train front has passed the resulting location (regards a)/ time (regards b)

¶927.content: - 3.8.2.4 The parameters given by the RBC also define whether the MA request shall be repeated until a new MA is received or not and if so, the time between each repetition.

¶928.content: - 3.8.2.5 The given data shall be valid until new MA request parameters are given from the RBC.

¶929.content: - 3.8.2.6 In case no MA request parameters are stored on-board and following an MA request no MA has been received, the request shall be repeated with a repetition cycle according to a fixed value (see appendix).

¶930.content: - 3.8.2.7 In level 2/3: an MA request shall be sent to the RBC when the driver selects start.

¶931.content: - 3.8.2.7.1 In level 0, 1, NTC: if a level 2/3 transition is announced and a communication session is already established, an MA request shall be sent to the RBC when the information "Track ahead free up to level 2/3 transition location" is received from balise group.

¶932.content: - 3.8.2.7.2 In level 0, 1, NTC: the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment shall also inform the RBC about the identity of the level 2/3 transition location balise group, as received through the information "Track ahead free up to level 2/3 transition location".

¶933.content: - 3.8.2.7.3 In  level  2/3:  An  MA  request  shall  be  sent  to  the  RBC  when  any  part  of  the  track description is deleted according to A.3.4, except for situations a, b, f, k.

¶934.content: - 3.8.2.8 Together with the MA request the on-board shall inform the RBC about the reason(s) why the MA request is sent:

¶936.content: - Time before reaching perturbation location reached,

¶937.content: -  Time before a section timer or the LOA speed timer expires reached,

¶938.content: - The track description has been deleted,

¶939.content: - Track ahead free up to level 2/3 transition location.

¶941.content: - 3.8.3.1 The distance to the End of Movement Authority can be composed of several sections.

¶942.content: - 3.8.3.2 For each section composing the MA the following information shall be given;

¶944.content: - Optionally, Section time-out value and distance from beginning of section to Section timer stop location

¶945.content: - 3.8.3.3 In addition, it shall be possible to define for the End Section of the MA:

¶946.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶947.content: - End Section time-out value and distance from the End Section timer start location to the end of the last section

¶948.content: - Danger  point  information  (distance  from  end  of  section  to  danger  point,  release speed related to danger point)

¶949.content: -  Overlap  information  (distance  from  end  of  section  to  end  of  overlap,  time-out, distance from Overlap timer start location to end of section, release speed related to overlap)

¶950.content: - 3.8.3.3.1 Note: If only one section is given in the MA it is regarded as the End Section.

¶951.content: - 3.8.3.4 The Section timer stop location shall be inside of the corresponding section.

¶952.content: - 3.8.3.4.1 Note:  the  End  Section  and  Overlap  timer  start  locations  may  be  outside  their corresponding section. One example can be seen referring to figure 22c: An infill MA towards  a  signal  at  stop  will  replace  the  previous  End  Section  by  a  new  short  End Section  starting  at  the  infill  location  reference  and  ending  at  the  next  main  signal, however the End Section and Overlap timer start locations still have to be consistent with  the  Interlocking  timer  start  locations.  Another  example  is  when  a  timer  start location is in rear of the LRBG.

¶953.content: - 3.8.3.5 In level 3, no time-outs shall be used.

¶954.content: - 3.8.3.5.1 Note:  For  level  3  functionality  the  split  of  responsibility  between  the  RBC  and  the Interlocking has to be considered. Time-out of routes and related overlap can not be based on train position reports since the position report can be delayed compared to

¶956.content: the  real  position  of  the  train.  The  route  release  and  revocation  of  routes  can  not  be carried out by the interlocking until permitted by the RBC.

¶957.content: - 3.8.3.6 When an MA is transmitted by a balise group, the length of the first section shall refer to the balise co-ordinate system of that balise group.

¶958.content: - 3.8.3.7 In case a main signal is at danger in level 1, the first section shall give the distance from  the  balise  group  at  the  main  signal  to  the  location  of  the  main  signal,  i.e.  the distance to EOA is given. Where available, information concerning danger point and overlap for this EOA may also be given.

¶959.content: - 3.8.3.7.1 Justification:  The  balise  group  is  not  necessarily  placed  at  the  same  location  as  the signal and thus an infill message (which includes the same information as the balise group at the main signal) could change the location of the EOA to a position closer to the train.

¶960.content: - 3.8.3.8 Note: In case the main signal is at danger in level 1, the on-board will supervise the given distance (specified in section 3.8.3.7) as the distance to EOA.

¶961.content: - 3.8.3.9 When an MA is transmitted by radio from the RBC, the length of the first section shall refer to the balise co-ordinate system of the LRBG given in the same message.

¶962.content: - 3.8.3.10 It shall be possible to give the length of a section to any location in the track.

¶963.content: - 3.8.3.10.1  Note:  A  section  can  cover  several  blocks  and  is  not  restricted  to  block  ends  (see figures).

¶964.content: Figure 18: Distance to End of Movement Authority when no time-outs are needed

¶965.content: # Figure  19  :  Distance  to  End  of  Movement  Authority  when  time-outs  might  be needed

¶966.content: - 3.8.3.11 In  moving  block  operation  the  MA  shall  never  exceed  the  min  safe  rear  end  of  the preceding train.

¶967.content: Figure 20: MA in moving block operation.

¶968.content: # 3.8.4 Use of the MA on board the train

¶970.content: - 3.8.4.1.1 The  End  Section  timer  shall  be  started  on-board  when  the  train  passes  the  End Section timer start location given by trackside with its max safe front end.

¶971.content: - 3.8.4.1.2 When the End Section timer value becomes greater than the time-out value given by trackside, the following shall apply:

¶972.content: - The  EOA/LOA  shall  be  withdrawn  to  the  current  position  of  the  train.  Refer  to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly;

¶973.content: - if any, a non zero target speed value at the End of Movement Authority shall be set to zero (i.e. an LOA at the End of Movement Authority becomes an EOA withdrawn to the current position of the train).

¶974.content: - 3.8.4.1.3 In case no End Section timer is running when the on-board receives a new MA with an End Section timer start location in rear of the max safe front end of the train, the onboard shall consider that the End Section timer value became greater than its time-out value and apply 3.8.4.1.2.

¶975.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶976.content: - 3.8.4.1.3.1 Justification:  in  this  case  the  train  is  already  beyond  the  timer  start  location, therefore  it  is  impossible  to  determine  when  that  location  was  crossed  and  so  what share of the time-out value has elapsed since the crossing event. The safe assumption is  to  consider that the time-out value has been exceeded and therefore that the End Section will soon be released by the Interlocking.

¶977.content: - 3.8.4.1.4 In case an End Section timer is already running when the on-board receives a new MA with an End Section timer start location in rear of the max safe front end of the train, the on-board shall keep the End Section timer running but replace the time-out value with the value received with the new MA.

¶978.content: - 3.8.4.1.4.1 Justification:  this  allows  repetition  of  the  MA  via  RBC  (e.g.  acknowledgment  of current MA was lost) without unintentionally affecting the End Section timer.

¶980.content: - 3.8.4.2.1 The on-board shall start a Section timer for each section:

¶981.content: - For Level 2: at the value of the time stamp of the message including the MA.

¶982.content: - For Level 1: at the time of passage over the first encountered balise of the balise group giving the MA.

¶983.content: - 3.8.4.2.1.1 Justification for b): This is to ensure that the timer is always started before or at the same  time  as  the  related  variable  information  is  received.  Thus  the  timer  start  is independent of in which balise the variable information is given.

¶984.content: - 3.8.4.2.2 When  a  Section  timer  value  becomes  greater  than  the  time-out  value  given  by trackside, the following shall apply:

¶985.content: - the  EOA/LOA  and  the  SvL  shall  be  withdrawn  to  the  entry  point  of  the  revoked section. Refer to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly;

¶986.content: - the National/ Default Value of the Release Speed shall apply ;

¶987.content: -  if any, a non zero target speed value at the End of Movement Authority shall be set to zero (i.e. an LOA at the End of Movement Authority becomes an EOA withdrawn to the entry point of the revoked section).

¶989.content: - 3.8.4.2.3 The Section timer shall be stopped when the min safe front end of the train has passed the associated Section timer stop location.

¶990.content: # 3.8.4.3 Time-out  of  the  speed  associated  with  the  End  of  Movement  Authority  (LOA speed time out)

¶991.content: - 3.8.4.3.1 The on-board shall start a timer for the LOA speed:

¶992.content: - For Level 2: at the value of the time stamp of the message including the MA.

¶993.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶994.content: - For Level 1: at the time of passage over the first encountered balise of the balise group giving the MA.

¶995.content: - 3.8.4.3.1.1 Justification for b): This is to ensure that the timer is always started before or at the same  time  as  the  related  variable  information  is  received.  Thus  the  timer  start  is independent of in which balise the variable information is given.

¶996.content: - 3.8.4.3.2 When the LOA speed timer value becomes greater than the time-out value given by trackside,  the  speed  associated  with  the  LOA  shall  be  set  to  zero  (i.e.  the  Limit  of Authority becomes an End of Authority and the SvL is defined on-board according to 3.8.4.5). Refer to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly.

¶998.content: - 3.8.4.4.1 The Overlap timer shall be started on-board when the train passes the Overlap timer start  location  given  by  trackside  with  its  max  safe  front  end.  The  timer  shall  be considered as started even if a time-out value 'infinite' is given.

¶999.content: - 3.8.4.4.2 When  the  Overlap  timer  value  becomes  greater  than  the  time-out  value  given  by trackside, the following shall apply:

¶1000.content: - the  overlap  information  shall  be  deleted  and  the  Supervised  Location  shall  be determined in accordance with 3.8.4.5. Refer to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly.

¶1001.content: - the release speed associated with the Overlap shall be deleted

¶1002.content: -  if any, a non zero target speed value at the End of Movement Authority shall be set to zero (i.e. an LOA at the End of Movement Authority becomes an EOA).

¶1003.content: - 3.8.4.4.3 If the train comes to a standstill after the Overlap timer has been started, the on-board shall consider that the Overlap timer value became greater than its time-out value and shall apply 3.8.4.4.2.

¶1004.content: - 3.8.4.4.4 In  case  no  Overlap  timer  is  running  when  the  on-board  receives  a  new  MA  with  an Overlap timer start location in rear of the max safe front end of the train, the on-board shall consider that the Overlap timer value became greater than its time-out value and shall apply 3.8.4.4.2.

¶1005.content: - 3.8.4.4.4.1 Justification:  in  this  case  the  train  is  already  beyond  the  timer  start  location, therefore  it  is  impossible  to  determine  when  that  location  was  crossed  and  so  what share of the time-out value has elapsed since the crossing event. The safe assumption is  to  consider  that  the  time-out  value  has  been  exceeded  and  therefore  that  the Overlap will soon be released by the Interlocking.

¶1006.content: - 3.8.4.4.5 In case an Overlap timer is already running when the on-board receives a new MA with an Overlap timer start location in rear of the max safe front end of the train, the on-

¶1007.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1008.content: board shall keep the Overlap timer running but replace the Overlap time-out value with the value received with the new MA.

¶1009.content: - 3.8.4.4.5.1 Justification:  this  allows  repetition  of  the  MA  via  RBC  (e.g.  acknowledgment  of current MA was lost) without unintentionally affecting the Overlap timer.

¶1011.content: - 3.8.4.5.1 The Supervised Location (SvL) shall be defined on-board as:

¶1012.content: - the end of overlap (if any and before time-out).

¶1013.content: - if not, the Danger Point (if any).

¶1014.content: -  if not, the End Of Authority.

¶1015.content: - 3.8.4.5.2 As long as a Limit of Authority is supervised, no SvL shall be defined on-board.

¶1017.content: - 3.8.4.6.1 An MA given by an infill device is called an infill MA.

¶1018.content: - 3.8.4.6.2 An infill MA shall be evaluated on-board only if the on-board equipment is in FS or LS mode.

¶1019.content: - 3.8.4.6.3 The  infill  information  shall  include  the  identity  of  the  balise  group  at  the  next  main signal i.e. the identity of the balise group giving the information that is transmitted in advance by the infill device.

¶1020.content: - 3.8.4.6.4 An infill  MA shall be evaluated on-board only if the linking information, regarding the main signal balise group to which it refers, is available.

¶1021.content: - 3.8.4.6.5 The on-board shall start a Section timer for each section beyond the next main signal:

¶1022.content: - When the infill information is received from a balise group at the time of passing the first encountered balise of the infill balise group.

¶1023.content: - When the infill information is received from a loop at the time of receiving the loop message.

¶1024.content: -  When the infill information is received from a radio infill unit at the value of the time stamp of the radio infill message including the MA.

¶1026.content: - 3.8.5.1 A new MA shall replace a previously received MA in the following ways:

¶1027.content: - When the new MA is given from a balise group at a main signal (i.e. not infill information) or from the RBC all data included in the previous MA shall be replaced by the new data.

¶1028.content: - When the new MA is given as infill information all data beyond the announced balise group at the next main signal shall be replaced.

¶1029.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1030.content: - 3.8.5.1.1 Note: This refers to all information included in the MA as listed in section 3.8.1.1 and the Signalling related speed restriction (see section 3.11.6).

¶1031.content: - 3.8.5.1.2 When an infill MA is received, the on-board shall start a new MA section at the infill location reference, i.e. the balise group at the next main signal (see 3.6.2.3.1).

¶1032.content: - 3.8.5.1.3 If the SvL defined from the new MA is closer than the one supervised with the former MA, this shall be considered by the on-board equipment as an MA shortening. Refer to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly.

¶1033.content: - 3.8.5.1.4 If  a  new  MA  defines  an  SvL  while  the  on-board  was  supervising  an  LOA,  this  shall always be considered by the on-board equipment as an MA shortening regardless of the  SvL  location.  Refer  to  appendix  A.3.4  for  the  exhaustive  list  of  location  based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly.

¶1034.content: - 3.8.5.2 It  shall  be  possible  to  update the length of  an MA section by means of repositioning information contained in a balise group message (see section 3.8.5.3).

¶1035.content: - 3.8.5.2.1 Note: The concerned MA section need not be the end section.

¶1036.content: - 3.8.5.2.2 Upon  reception  of  repositioning  information and  only  if linking  information  has announced a following balise group as unknown but containing repositioning information, the on-board shall update the length of the current MA section in which the train front end is.

¶1037.content: - 3.8.5.2.3 A balise group message containing a movement authority shall not contain repositioning information for the same direction.

¶1038.content: - 3.8.5.2.3.1 Note: It is possible to combine repositioning with an infill MA.

¶1039.content: - 3.8.5.2.4 The reception of repositioning information or of a new MA with an LOA shall not be considered as an MA shortening by the on-board equipment.

¶1041.content: - 3.8.5.3.1 Note: In the following examples on how to update an MA are given. The examples are not exhaustive.

¶1042.content: - 3.8.5.3.2 Example: Extension of MA via a main balise group in Level 1

¶1043.content: -  by giving a new longer section, see Figure 21a

¶1044.content: -  by giving a first section to the same location as in the previous MA and a second section, see Figure 21b

¶1045.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1046.content: Figure 21a: Extension of an MA in Level 1, one section in the new MA

¶1047.content: Figure 21b: Extension of an MA in level 1, two sections in the new MA

¶1048.content: - 3.8.5.3.3 Example:  MA  update  via  infill  information  in  Level  1.  (Refer  to  section  3.6.2.3  for location reference of infill information)

¶1049.content: -  MA extension, by giving two new sections, see Figure 22a

¶1052.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1053.content: Figure 22a: Extension of an MA with Infill information

¶1054.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1055.content: Figure 22b: Shortening of an MA with Infill information

¶1056.content: Figure 22c: Repetition of an MA with Infill information

¶1057.content: # 3.8.5.3.4 Example: Extension of MA in Levels 2 and 3

¶1058.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1059.content: -  by using the same LRBG as in previous MA, see Figure 23a

¶1060.content: -  by using a new LRBG, see Figure 23b

¶1061.content: - 3.8.5.3.5 Example:  Extension  of  MA  in  level  1  using  a  balise  group  containing  repositioning information.

¶1062.content: - 3.8.5.3.5.1 Note: In some existing systems, information about the locked route is not complete.

¶1063.content: - 3.8.5.3.5.2 History of the situation (refer to the figure below):

¶1064.content: - Signal A gives an aspect to proceed up to signal Cx because it has received information about the locked route.

¶1065.content: - Signal A can determine whether track 3 or track 1 / 2 is locked but is unable to distinguish between track 1 and 2.

¶1066.content: -  In the situation described the route is set to track 1 or 2.

¶1067.content: Figure 23a: Extension of an MA in level 2/3, using same LRBG

¶1068.content: Figure 23b: Extension of an MA in Level 2/3, using a new LRBG

¶1069.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1070.content: Figure 24: Information on set route not complete at signal A

¶1071.content: - 3.8.5.3.5.3 In balise group A the following information is given:

¶1072.content: - The most restrictive track description from all routes (which could be a combination from the routes);

¶1073.content: - The  linking  distance  given  to  the  farthest  balise  group  containing  repositioning information, the identification of the repositioning balise group is not known;

¶1074.content: -  For a given aspect of signal A, the most restrictive MA from all routes (the shortest sections  from  the  routes  and  the  lowest  target  speed  at  the  End  of  Movement Authority);

¶1075.content: - If some sections are time limited, the most restrictive timer.

¶1076.content: - 3.8.5.3.5.4 Balise groups B (B1 or B2) give the following static information:

¶1078.content: - Linking to the next balise group C

¶1079.content: -  The distance to the end of the current section (i.e. the distance to the end of section B1 - C1, or the distance to the end of section B2 - C2)

¶1080.content: - The track description related to this track.

¶1081.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1082.content: Figure 25: Information contained in A and B1 (for clarity purposes, only SSPs are drawn but the procedure has to be applied for all track description)

¶1083.content: # 3.8.6 Co-operative shortening of MA (Level 2 and 3 only)

¶1084.content: - 3.8.6.1 It shall be possible to shorten a given MA using a special procedure between on-board equipment and RBC. The procedure is as follows:

¶1085.content: - The RBC proposes a new MA with an EOA closer to the train than the current EOA/LOA, optionally with a mode profile.

¶1086.content: - The ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment shall check the train front end position versus the Indication supervision limit of the proposed shortened MA.

¶1087.content: -  If it is in rear, the on-board equipment shall accept the new MA.

¶1088.content: -  If it is in advance, the request shall be rejected and the previously received MA remains valid

¶1089.content: -  The RBC shall be informed about the decision.

¶1090.content: - 3.8.6.2 If the request from the RBC is granted by the on-board, refer to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information, which shall be deleted accordingly.

¶1091.content: # 3.9 Means to transmit Infill information (Level 1 only)

¶1093.content: - 3.9.1.1 It shall be possible to transmit infill information to the on-board equipment using

¶1097.content: - 3.9.1.1.1 If  the  information  transmitted  by  Balise  groups,  Euroloops,  and  Radio  includes  infill information, those devices are also identified as infill devices.

¶1098.content: - 3.9.1.2 The  principle  used  for  the  infill  information  shall  be  the  same  independent  of transmission media.

¶1099.content: - 3.9.1.3 If the on-board system is not equipped with the infill transmission media as requested by the announcement balise group, the announcement information shall be ignored by the  on-board  equipment  and  the  train  shall  proceed  according  to  the  previously received information.

¶1100.content: - 3.9.1.4 Note: No additional description is needed for infill by balise group (other than already covered in previous chapters).

¶1102.content: - 3.9.2.1 An End Of Loop Marker (EOLM) is by definition a device to mark the beginning or the end of a loop. When receiving this information, the on-board equipment knows that it is entering/leaving  a  track  equipped  with  a  loop.  In  unidirectional  applications  it  is possible to have an EOLM only at the entry side of a loop.

¶1103.content: - 3.9.2.2 Balise groups shall be used as EOLMs. They act as an EOLM by sending the EOLM information to the passing train.

¶1104.content: - 3.9.2.3 EOLMs have an orientation that is identical to the balise group orientation. The general rules for balise orientation therefore also apply to EOLMs.

¶1105.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1106.content: - 3.9.2.4 EOLM information might be contained in a balise group that contains other information.

¶1107.content: - 3.9.2.5 The EOLM shall send the identity of the announced loop.

¶1108.content: - 3.9.2.6 The on-board shall only accept information coming from the loop with the announced identity.

¶1111.content: - 3.9.2.9 The  following  information  shall  be  sent  in  advance  by  the  EOLM  to  prepare  for reception of the loop information:

¶1112.content: -  Loop identity used to identify the loop.

¶1113.content: -  Key to select the spread spectrum key necessary to receive the loop telegrams.

¶1114.content: -  Distance to the loop giving the distance from the EOLM to the location from where on loop messages can be received.

¶1115.content: -  Length  of  the  loop  giving  the  length  of  the  loop  over  which  messages  can  be received.

¶1116.content: -  Indicator  telling  the  on-board  whether  the  orientation  of  the  loop  is  identical  or reverse to the orientation of the announcing EOLM.

¶1117.content: - 3.9.2.10 The on-board shall be prepared to receive messages from the Euroloop after passing the EOLM.

¶1118.content: - 3.9.2.11 When  the  on-board  equipment  reads  the  next  main  signal  balise  group  or  when  it detects  that  the  next  main  signal  balise  group  was  missed,  new  infill  information possibly received from the loop shall be ignored.

¶1119.content: - 3.9.2.12 The  distances  given  in  an  EOLM  (distance  to  loop,  length  of  loop)  are  used  for diagnostic purpose only. They shall therefore not be used to restrict reception of loop telegrams to specific locations.

¶1121.content: - 3.9.3.1 In  level  1  areas  it  shall  be  possible  to  send  to  the  on-board  equipment  orders  to establish/terminate a communication session with a radio infill unit.

¶1122.content: - 3.9.3.2 The orders shall be sent via balise groups or via Radio Infill units.

¶1123.content: - 3.9.3.3 The order to establish a communication session shall be ignored:

¶1125.content: - If the on-board equipment does not include radio.

¶1126.content: - 3.9.3.4 If  the  on-board  equipment  includes  radio,  the  communication  session  shall  be established using the same protocols and interfaces as for Level 2/3 operations.

¶1127.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1128.content: - 3.9.3.5 If the order to establish a communication session with a radio infill unit sent via balise groups is  received,  the  on-board  equipment  shall,  once  the  location  indicated  in  the order is reached:

¶1129.content: - terminate  any  existing  communication  session(s)  with  RIU(s)  not  indicated  in  the order

¶1130.content: - as soon as a new communication session can be handled, establish a communication session with the RIU indicated in the order

¶1134.content: - 3.9.3.6 If the order to establish a communication session with a radio infill unit sent via Radio Infill unit is received, the on-board equipment shall:

¶1135.content: - If only one RIU communication session is ongoing, maintain the existing communication session and establish a new one with the RIU indicated in the order.

¶1136.content: - If  two  RIU  communication  sessions  are  ongoing,  maintain  the  communication session related to the current infill area, terminate the other one and establish a new one with the RIU indicated in the order.

¶1137.content: - 3.9.3.6.1 Exception  (degraded  situation):  if  the  on-board  can  handle  only  one  communication session, the order shall be ignored.

¶1138.content: - 3.9.3.7 A  Radio  Infill  Unit  shall  not  initiate  a  communication  session  with  an  on-board equipment.

¶1139.content: - 3.9.3.8 The order to establish/terminate a communication session sent via balise groups shall be sent together with the following radio infill area information:

¶1140.content: - Location  where  to  perform  the  action  (referred  to  the  balise  group  containing  the order).Note:  if  the  action  is  to  establish  a  communication  session,  this  location marks the beginning of the Radio Infill Area.

¶1141.content: - Next  main  signal  balise  group  identifier  (ignored  by  the  on-board  if  the  action  is Terminate communication session).Note: the reference location of this balise group marks the end of the Radio Infill Area.

¶1142.content: - 3.9.3.8.1 The  order  to  establish/terminate  a  communication  session  sent  via  Radio  Infill  units (see 3.5.3.6) shall not be sent together with any radio infill area information.

¶1143.content: - 3.9.3.9 The  establishment  of  a  communication  session  for  radio  infill  shall  not  change  the operational level of the on-board i.e. the information in the balise group shall be taken into account as usual in level 1.

¶1144.content: - 3.9.3.10 The on-board equipment shall inform the radio infill unit

¶1145.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1146.content: - As soon as the location indicated in the order sent via balise groups is passed (i.e. entry of the train in the infill area)

¶1147.content: - As soon as the next main signal balise group indicated in the order sent via balise groups is read or the on-board equipment detects that it was missed.

¶1148.content: - 3.9.3.11 The information sent to the radio infill unit by the on-board equipment shall include

¶1149.content: - Train identity (ETCS-ID of the on-board equipment)

¶1151.content: -  Identifier of the next main signal balise group

¶1154.content: - The train identity is used for conformity with other train to track messages

¶1155.content: - The  identifier  of  the  next  main  signal  balise  group  allows  the  radio  infill  unit  to identify safely where the train is going, even in the case of a points area

¶1156.content: - 3.9.3.12 As soon as the radio infill unit is informed that a train has entered an infill area under its responsibility, it shall

¶1157.content: - Terminate a possible previous sending  of infill information to the  on-board equipment, AND

¶1158.content: - Send cyclically the infill information corresponding to the message currently sent by the  next  main  signal  balise  group  indicated  in  the  information  from  the  on-board equipment.

¶1159.content: - 3.9.3.12.1  Justification: case a) refers to the possibility that a report from the on-board equipment, after having passed the previous main signal, was lost.

¶1160.content: - 3.9.3.12.2  Note: A Radio infill unit  may manage several signals, thus several Radio Infill Areas (see Figure 25a)

¶1161.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1162.content: -  NMBG = Next Main signal Balise Group

¶1164.content: -  RIU2 manages Radio Infill Area 2 and 3

¶1166.content: # Figure 25a: Line equipped with radio infill. Example of radio infill area information transmitted by balise.

¶1167.content: - 3.9.3.13 The  radio  infill  unit  shall  terminate  the  sending  of  infill  information  as  soon  as information  is  received,  that  the  on-board  equipment  has  read  the  next  main  signal balise group indicated in the order or that the on-board equipment has detected that it was missed.

¶1168.content: - 3.9.3.14 The radio infill unit shall evaluate the time stamp according to the principles of section 3.16.3.2.

¶1169.content: - The on-board equipment shall check the consistency of radio infill data, according to the principles of section 3.16.3.1 and 3.16.3.3.

¶1170.content: - 3.9.3.15 When  the  on-board  equipment  reads  the  next  main  signal  balise  group  or  when  it detects  that  the  next  main  signal  balise  group  was  missed,  new  infill  information related to this balise group possibly received shall be ignored.

¶1171.content: - 3.9.3.16 The  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  terminate  the  communication  session according to the orders received from the trackside (balise group or Radio Infill units) or when the level 1 is left.

¶1175.content: - 3.10.1.1 Emergency messages shall be sent individually to each on-board equipment, either as high priority data or as normal priority data of the same radio connection, as described

¶1176.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1177.content: in  Euroradio  specification.  Only  emergency  messages  can  be  sent  as  high  priority data, not their acknowledgement or their revocation.

¶1178.content: - 3.10.1.1.1  Justification: In case of hazardous events, it is possible to use high priority data in the radio connection between RBC and on-board equipment to get a quick reaction.

¶1179.content: - 3.10.1.2 An emergency message shall contain an identifier decided by the trackside.

¶1180.content: - 3.10.1.3 The same identifier shall be used in case the emergency message is repeated.

¶1181.content: - 3.10.1.3.1  If  the  on-board  receives a new message with the same identifier it shall replace the previous one.

¶1182.content: - 3.10.1.4 Each emergency message to an on-board equipment shall be acknowledged, using the corresponding emergency message identification number.

¶1183.content: - 3.10.1.4.1  Note:  This  acknowledgement  informs  the  RBC  about  the  use  of  the  emergency message by on-board equipment and is independent from the general acknowledgement for track-to-train messages, as specified in section 3.16.3.5.

¶1185.content: - 3.10.2.1 It  shall  be  possible  to  stop  a  train  with  a  conditional  or  an  unconditional  emergency stop message.

¶1186.content: - 3.10.2.2 A  conditional  emergency  stop  message  shall  contain  the  information  of  a  new  stop location, referred to the LRBG. In case, when receiving this message

¶1187.content: -  the train has already passed with its min safe front end the new stop location, the emergency stop message shall be rejected and the RBC shall be informed.

¶1188.content: -  the train has not yet passed with its min safe front end the new stop location, the emergency stop message shall be accepted, however this location shall be used by the  onboard  to  define  a  new  EOA/SvL  only  if  not  beyond  the  current  EOA/LOA. Refer to appendix A.3.4 for the exhaustive list of location based information stored on-board, which shall be deleted accordingly.

¶1189.content: - 3.10.2.3 When receiving an unconditional emergency stop message the train shall be tripped immediately.

¶1190.content: - 3.10.2.4 New movement authority received after any accepted emergency stop message and before the emergency message has been revoked, shall be rejected.

¶1194.content: - 3.10.3.1 The  revocation  message  shall  refer  to  the  identity  of  the  concerned  emergency message.

¶1195.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1196.content: - 3.10.3.2 The  revocation  messages  shall  be  acknowledged  by  the  on-board  equipment, according to the general acknowledgement procedure (see section 3.16.3.5)

¶1197.content: - 3.10.3.3 The revocation of an emergency message shall have no effect on the management of other emergency messages possibly received.

¶1201.content: - 3.11.1.1 The permitted speed at which the train is allowed to travel shall be limited to different kinds of Static Speed Restrictions.

¶1202.content: - 3.11.1.2 A  Static  Speed  Restriction  shall  be  handled  in  the  same  way  independent  of  ETCS level.

¶1204.content: - 3.11.2.1 Static Speed Restrictions are imposed by the trackside infrastructure, the train characteristics, the signalling and the mode of the on-board equipment.

¶1205.content: - 3.11.2.2 There are eleven categories of Static Speed Restrictions:

¶1212.content: - STM Max speed (for details refer to Subset-035)

¶1213.content: - STM System speed (for details refer to Subset-035)

¶1216.content: -  Speed  restriction  to  ensure  a  given  permitted  braking  distance  (PBD  SR)  (see 3.11.11)

¶1217.content: - 3.11.2.3 The Static Speed Restriction categories  are independent of each other. This means that  one  speed  restriction  category  cannot  affect,  nor  be  affected  by,  any  other category of Static Speed Restrictions.

¶1218.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1219.content: Figure 26: Example of Static Speed Restriction categories on a piece of track .

¶1220.content: - 3.11.2.4 Depending on the type of Static Speed Restriction train length may have to be used to ensure that the full length of the train has passed a Static Speed Restriction discontinuity before a speed increase shall be taken into account.

¶1224.content: - 3.11.3.1.1  The  Static  Speed  Profile  (SSP)  is  a  description  of  the  fixed  speed  restrictions  of  a given piece of track. The speed restrictions can be related to e.g. maximum line speed, curves, points, tunnel profiles, bridges.

¶1225.content: - 3.11.3.1.2  The Static Speed Profile is based on factors, which are both track and train dependent. The  relationship  between  track  and  train  characteristics  determines  the  individual Static Speed Profile for each train.

¶1226.content: - 3.11.3.1.3  It shall be possible for every element (distance between two discontinuities) of a static speed  profile  to  define,  if  a  transition  to  a  higher  speed  limit  than  the  speed  limit specified for this element is permitted before the complete train has left the element.

¶1228.content: - 3.11.3.2.1  It shall be possible to transmit several Static Speed Profile Categories; one Basic SSP category and specific SSP categories related to the international train categories.

¶1229.content: - 3.11.3.2.1.1 The specific SSP categories are decomposed into two types:

¶1230.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1231.content: - The 'Cant Deficiency' SSP categories: the cant deficiency value assigned to one category  shall  define  the  maximum  speed,  determined  by  suspension  design,  at which a particular train can traverse a curve and thus can be used to set a specific speed limit in a curve with regards to this category.

¶1232.content: - The  'other  specific'  SSP  categories:  it  groups  all  other  specific  SSP  categories corresponding to the other international train categories

¶1233.content: - 3.11.3.2.1.2 Whenever  the  type  of  specific  SSP  category  is  not  explicitly  specified  in  the following  requirements,  it  shall  be  interpreted  as  being  applicable  for  both  types  of specific SSP categories.

¶1234.content: - 3.11.3.2.2  For each part of the Static Speed Profile, the ERTMS/ETCS trackside shall:

¶1235.content: - always  give  the  Basic  SSP,  which  shall  be  considered  as  the  default  'Cant Deficiency' SSP

¶1236.content: - optionally give one or more specific SSPs

¶1237.content: -  specify,  for  each  'other  specific'  SSP,  whether  it  replaces  or  not  the  'Cant Deficiency' SSP as selected by the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment according to 3.11.3.2.3

¶1238.content: - 3.11.3.2.3  For each part of the Static Speed Profile, the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment shall select  the  SSP  best  suiting  its  'Cant  Deficiency'  train  category,  according  to  the following order of preference:

¶1239.content: - if available, the 'Cant Deficiency' SSP matching its 'Cant Deficiency' train category, OR

¶1240.content: - if available, the 'Cant Deficiency' SSP with the highest Cant Deficiency value below the value of its 'Cant Deficiency' train category, OR

¶1244.content: - 3.11.3.2.5  'Other Specific' SSP categories not relevant to the current train shall be ignored.

¶1245.content: - 3.11.3.2.6  For each part of the Static Speed Profile, the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment in a train belonging to at least one or more 'other international' train categories shall use the most restrictive speed amongst:

¶1246.content: - the  '  Cant  Deficiency'  SSP  as  selected  in  3.11.3.2.3,  only  if  none  of  the  'other specific' SSP  categories  matching  the  train  categories  replaces  the  '  Cant Deficiency' SSP, AND

¶1247.content: - all  the  'other  specific'  SSP  categories  matching  the  'other  international'  train categories.

¶1249.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1250.content: - 3.11.3.3.1  A maximum of 31 train categories is defined to match the SSP categories. 16 'Cant Deficiency' train categories and 15 'other international' train categories.

¶1251.content: - 3.11.3.3.2  A train shall always belong to one and only one 'Cant Deficiency' train category and may optionally belong to one or more 'other international' train categories.

¶1252.content: - 3.11.3.3.3  The train category(ies) to which a train belongs is a part of its Train Data.

¶1254.content: - 3.11.4.1 It shall be possible to define an Axle load Speed Profile as a non-continuous profile.

¶1255.content: - 3.11.4.2 For each section with a speed restriction due to axle load, the different speed value(s) and  for  which  minimum  axle  load  category  this  speed  value(s)  applies  shall  be specified.

¶1256.content: - 3.11.4.2.1  Note:  Different  speed  restrictions  depending  on  the  axle  load  category  can  be applicable to the same distance.

¶1257.content: - 3.11.4.3 The  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  consider  the  most  restrictive  speed restriction that is associated with any axle load category lower than, or equal to that of the train.

¶1258.content: - 3.11.4.4 For trains with an axle load category lower than the minimum axle load category given in  the  profile,  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  not  consider  any  speed restriction due to axle load.

¶1259.content: - 3.11.4.5 The initial state for Axle load Speed Profile shall be 'no restriction due to axle load'.

¶1260.content: - 3.11.4.6 Whether a speed increase after the axle load speed restriction shall be delayed with train length, shall be determined by the axle load speed profile information sent to the on-board equipment.

¶1262.content: - 3.11.5.1 The temporary speed restriction is defined in order to enable a separate category of track infrastructure speed restriction, which can be used for working areas etc.

¶1263.content: - 3.11.5.2 All Temporary Speed Restrictions are independent of each other. This means that an individual  Temporary  Speed  Restriction  cannot  affect,  nor  be  affected  by,  any  other individual Temporary Speed Restriction.

¶1264.content: - 3.11.5.3 Whether a speed increase after the temporary speed restriction shall be delayed with train length, shall be determined by the temporary speed restriction information sent to the on-board equipment.

¶1265.content: - 3.11.5.4 When two or more temporary speed restrictions overlap, the most restrictive speed of the overlapping temporary speed restrictions shall be used in the area of overlap.

¶1266.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1267.content: - 3.11.5.5 Each Temporary Speed Restriction shall have an identity to make it possible to revoke the  Temporary  Speed  Restriction  using  its  identity.  The  speed  restriction  shall  be revoked immediately when revocation is received from trackside, without delay for the train length.

¶1268.content: - 3.11.5.6 It  shall  be  possible  to  identify  whether  a  Temporary  Speed  Restriction  is  possible  to revoke or not.

¶1269.content: - 3.11.5.7 A new Temporary Speed Restriction shall not replace a previously received Temporary Speed Restriction with another identity.

¶1270.content: - 3.11.5.8 Temporary Speed Restrictions shall only be revoked on request from the trackside.

¶1271.content: - 3.11.5.9 If the on-board equipment receives a new Temporary Speed Restriction (TSR) with the same identity as an already received TSR, the new Temporary Speed Restriction shall replace the previous one, except when the Temporary Speed Restriction is identified as non revocable in which case this shall be considered as an additional TSR.

¶1272.content: - 3.11.5.10 In case the train has changed its orientation any Temporary Speed Restriction shall be deleted (operational requirement: will be executed due to the mode change).

¶1274.content: - 3.11.5.12 It shall be possible for the RBC to order an ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment in Level 2 or 3 to reject revocable TSRs from balises.

¶1275.content: - 3.11.5.13 When ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment has accepted an order to reject revocable TSRs from balises, this inhibition  shall  be  stored  and shall be effective immediately, but only for revocable TSRs received from balises thereafter.

¶1276.content: - 3.11.5.14 The inhibition of revocable TSRs from balises shall be deleted if any of the following occurs:

¶1277.content: -  the communication session established with the RBC that ordered the inhibition is terminated, OR

¶1278.content: -  in case of RBC/RBC handover, the train front end crosses the RBC/RBC border.

¶1279.content: - 3.11.5.15 Note:  this  inhibition  may  be  useful  in  Level  1  /  Level  2  mixed  signalling  applications when the RBC has more precise information about restrictions than can be given from balises.  The  RBC  may  then  order  inhibition  of  revocable  TSRs  from  balises  and instead send more precise TSRs to the train.

¶1281.content: - 3.11.6.1 In level 1, it shall be possible to send to the on-board equipment a speed restriction with a value depending on the current state of signalling.

¶1282.content: - 3.11.6.2 This speed value shall be taken into account by the on-board equipment as soon as it is  received on-board, with the exception of a signalling related speed restriction from

¶1283.content: an  infill  device.  In  case  of  infill  information  the  speed  restriction  shall  be  taken  into account from the location reference of the balise group at the next main signal.

¶1284.content: - 3.11.6.3 The speed restriction shall be valid until a new signalling related speed restriction is received.

¶1285.content: - 3.11.6.3.1  If  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  switches  from  level  1  to  level  2/3,  the signalling related speed restriction shall remain valid until a L2/3 MA is accepted by the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment

¶1286.content: - 3.11.6.4 In case of a signal at danger the signalling related speed restriction shall have value zero,  which  shall  be  evaluated  by  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  not  as  a speed limit but as a train trip order.

¶1287.content: - 3.11.6.5 In  case  of  infill  information  the  signalling  related  speed  restriction  at  zero  shall  be ignored.

¶1288.content: - 3.11.6.5.1  Note: The infill information will also include an EOA at the next main signal that will be supervised according to the normal rules.

¶1290.content: - 3.11.7.1 The  value of the mode  related speed  restriction shall be determined  by  the corresponding national value or the corresponding default values if the national values are not applicable.

¶1291.content: - 3.11.7.1.1   Exception  1:  For  the  modes  On-sight,  Limited  Supervision  and  Shunting  the  speed limit  can  also  be  given  from  the  trackside.  The  speed  limit  given  from  the  trackside shall prevail over the National value and the default value.

¶1292.content: - 3.11.7.1.2  Exception  2:  For  the  mode  Reversing  there  is  no  National/Default  value.  The  speed limit is always given from trackside.

¶1293.content: - 3.11.7.1.3  Exception 3: For the mode Staff Responsible the speed limit can also be entered by the  driver.  The  speed  limit  given  by  the  driver  shall  prevail  over  the  National/Default value.

¶1295.content: - 3.11.8.1 It shall  be  possible  to  define  the  maximum  train  speed  related  to  the  actual performance and configuration of the train.

¶1297.content: - 3.11.9.1 It shall be possible to define a LX speed restriction when the train has to pass a non protected Level Crossing.

¶1299.content: - 3.11.10.1 While the 'override' function is active, the override speed limit (national /default value) shall be taken into account.

¶1300.content: # 3.11.11 Speed restriction to ensure permitted braking distance

¶1301.content: - 3.11.11.1 It  shall be possible for trackside to request the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment to calculate a speed restriction based on a permitted braking distance given by trackside.

¶1302.content: - 3.11.11.2 The order shall be given by means of a non-continuous profile defining:

¶1303.content: -  The start and end location for the speed restriction

¶1304.content: -  The permitted braking distance (PBD) used to calculate the speed restriction value

¶1305.content: -  Whether the permitted braking distance is to be achieved with the Service Brake or Emergency Brake

¶1306.content: -  A single gradient value applicable for the calculation

¶1307.content: - 3.11.11.3 The speed restriction shall be calculated when the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment receives  the  permitted  braking  distance  information  from  trackside,  and  shall  be  recalculated only if any of the inputs taken into account for the calculation of the speed restriction changes.

¶1308.content: - 3.11.11.4 The calculation of the speed restriction by the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment shall take into account that:

¶1309.content: -  The single gradient value received from trackside shall be compensated in value according to the rotating mass as defined in 3.13.4.3.

¶1310.content: -  The safe deceleration shall be computed as in 3.13.6.2.1 but without considering the adhesion profiles, the track conditions related to brake inhibition and the track conditions related to powerless section given by trackside.

¶1311.content: -  The  expected  deceleration  shall  be  computed  as  in  3.13.6.3.1  but  without considering the track conditions related to brake inhibition and the track conditions related to powerless section given by trackside.

¶1312.content: -  The  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  calculate  an  Emergency  Brake Deceleration  (EBD)  curve  based  on  the  safe  deceleration  and  that  reaches  zero speed at a distance equal to the permitted braking distance.

¶1313.content: -  If  the  permitted  braking  distance  is  to  be  achieved  with  the  service  brake,  the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment shall also calculate a Service Brake Deceleration  (SBD)  curve  based  on  the  expected  deceleration  and  that  reaches zero speed at a distance equal to the permitted braking distance.

¶1314.content: -  The estimated acceleration shall be set to 'zero'.

¶1315.content: -  If not inhibited by National Value, the compensation of the inaccuracy of the speed measurement shall be set to a value calculated from the PBD speed, as defined in SUBSET-041  §  5.3.1.2:  V_delta0PBD  =  f41(V_PBD  +  dV_EBI(V_PBD))  if  the

¶1316.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1317.content: permitted braking distance is to be achieved with the emergency brake; V_delta0PBD = f41(V_PBD + dV_SBI(V_PBD)) if the permitted braking distance is to be achieved with the service brake.

¶1318.content: -  The  train  will  travel  a  distance  from  the  last  encountered  balise  of  a  group  that provides  restrictive  information  until  initiating  the  brake  command.  This  travelled distance  shall  be  set  to  a  value  calculated  from  the  PBD  speed  considering  a processing delay (T41) equal to SUBSET-041 § 5.2.1.1.

¶1319.content: -  Regardless  of  how  the  service  brake  feedback  is  actually configured  (see 3.13.2.2.7.2), T_bs1 and T_bs2 (see 3.13.9.3.3) shall be defined as if the service brake feedback was not implemented.

¶1320.content: -  Regardless  of  how  the  traction  cut-off  interface  is  actually  configured  (see 3.13.2.2.8), T_traction (see 3.13.9.3.2) shall be defined as if the traction cut-off was not implemented.
