¶1321.content: - 3.11.11.4.1  Note: Knowing how the PBD speed restriction is computed by the ERTMS/ETCS onboard  equipment,  it  is  the  responsibility  of  the  trackside  to  set  the  appropriate permitted braking distance with regard to the risk of not initiating the brake command in due time when encountering a further balise group providing restrictive information. In  other  words,  if  deemed  necessary,  the  trackside  can include provisions based on the  characteristics  of  the  balise  group  providing  restrictive  information  e.g.  distances between  balises  in that group  with regards  to validity direction of transmitted information and balise group orientation, accuracy of balise location.

¶1322.content: - 3.11.11.4.2  Note: If the permitted braking distance is to be achieved with the service brake, it is the responsibility  of  the  trackside  to  also  consider  an  estimation  of  the  on-board  overreading and under-reading amounts at the time the brake command is initiated in order to  lower  the  likelihood  of  the  max  safe  front  end  of  the  train  reaching  the  EBI supervision limit.

¶1323.content: - 3.11.11.5 Note: Throughout the following formulas, all the distances marked with 'd' (lower case) are counted from a single arbitrary reference location.

¶1324.content: - 3.11.11.6 If  the  permitted  braking  distance  is  to  be  achieved  with  the  emergency  brake,  the ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  seek  the  PBD  speed  restriction  value (V_PBD) which satisfies the two following inequalities. The resulting value shall then be rounded down to the next lower multiple of 5km/h:

¶1329.content:

\text {with } D _ { b e c } = \left ( V _ { P B D } + d V _ { e b i } + V _ { d e l t a 0 P B D } \right ) \cdot \left ( T _ { t r a c t i o n } + T _ { b e r e m } \right )
$$

¶1330.content:

$$

¶1332.content: With   d VEBD being the EBD curve that reaches zero speed at PBD d

¶1334.content: If no speed value fulfils the above inequalities, then:

¶1336.content: - 3.11.11.7 If  the  permitted  braking  distance  is  to  be  achieved  with  the  service  brake,  the  PBD speed restriction (V_PBD) shall be equal to the most restrictive value amongst the one computed from  the  EBD  (see  3.11.11.8)  and  the  one  computed  from  the  SBD  (see 3.11.11.9). The resulting value shall then be rounded down to the next lower multiple of 5km/h.

¶1337.content: - 3.11.11.8 If  the  permitted  braking  distance  is  to  be  achieved  with  the  service  brake,  the ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  seek  the  PBDEBD  speed  restriction  value which satisfies the two following inequalities:

¶1339.content: With sbi dV as defined in 3.13.9.2.5 by substituting MRSP V with PBD V

$$

¶1342.content: With traction T and berem T as defined in 3.13.9.3.2

¶1343.content: With 2 bs T as defined in 3.13.9.3.3

¶1344.content: With PBD d being the permitted braking distance given by trackside

¶1345.content: With   d VEBD being the EBD curve that reaches zero speed at PBD d

¶1347.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1348.content: If no speed value fulfils the above inequalities, then:

¶1350.content: - 3.11.11.9 If  the  permitted  braking  distance  is  to  be  achieved  with  the  service  brake,  the ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall  seek  the  PBDSBD  speed  restriction  value which satisfies the two following inequalities:

¶1351.content:
$$
\ A B S \left \{ \left ( V _ { _ { P B D } } + d V _ { _ { s b i } } \right ) - \left ( V _ { _ { S B D } } \left ( d _ { _ { o f f s e t } } + \left ( V _ { _ { P B D } } + d V _ { _ { s b i } } \right ) \cdot T _ { _ { b s 1 } } \right ) \right ) \right \} \leq 1 k m / h
$$

¶1354.content: With sbi dV as defined in 3.13.9.2.5 by substituting MRSP V with PBD V

¶1355.content: With 1 bs T as defined in 3.13.9.3.3

¶1356.content: With PBD d being the permitted braking distance given by trackside

¶1357.content: With   d VSBD being the SBD curve that reaches zero speed at PBD d

$$

¶1359.content: If no speed value fulfils the above inequalities, then:

¶1361.content: - 3.11.11.10 Note:  The  method  chosen  (e.g.  iterative  algorithm)  to  compute  the  PBD  speed restriction(s) is an implementation issue.

¶1362.content: - 3.11.11.11 The initial state for Speed Restrictions to Ensure Permitted Braking Distance shall be 'no speed restriction'.

¶1364.content: - 3.11.12.1 The gradient information for a given piece of track shall be transmitted to the on-board equipment in form of a gradient profile.

¶1365.content: - 3.11.12.2 The gradient profile shall  be continuous, i.e., give a gradient value for each location within the piece of track covered by the profile.

¶1366.content: - 3.11.12.3 A gradient value shall be identified as a positive value for an uphill slope, and with a negative value for a downhill slope.

¶1367.content: - 3.11.12.4 The gradient profile shall contain the gradient information as a sequence of gradient values, constant between two defined locations each, see Figure 27.

¶1368.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1370.content: - 3.11.12.4.1  Note: The figure above symbolises the engineering process to provide the values of gradients.  Following  the  track  height,  the  track  must  be  split  in  segments  giving  for each segment a gradient value.

¶1371.content: - 3.11.12.5 It  shall  be  possible  via  balise  groups  to  send  to  the  on-board  equipment  a  default gradient  for  TSR,  to  be  used  for  the  parts  of  the  track  not  covered  by  the  gradient profile.

¶1372.content: - 3.11.12.6 The  Default  Gradient  for  TSR  stored  on-board  shall  be  valid  until  a  new  Default Gradient for TSR is received.

¶1375.content: - 3.12.1.1 The Track Condition function is used to inform the driver and/or the train of a condition in front of the train.

¶1376.content: - 3.12.1.2 A Track Condition shall be given as profile data (e.g. non-stopping area), i.e. start and end  of  the  data  is  given,  or  location  data  (e.g.  change  of  traction  system)  i.e.  start location given, depending on the type of track condition.

¶1377.content: - 3.12.1.2.1  The  starting  point  of  a  profile  type  track  condition  shall  be  evaluated  taking  into account the max safe front end of the train, the end of the profile taking into account the  min  safe  rear  end  of  the  train.  Location  type  data  shall  be  evaluated  taking  into account the max safe front of the train.

¶1378.content: - 3.12.1.2.1.1 Note:  The  timing  of  output  data  to  control  train  equipment  (e.g.  pantograph)  is application specific.

¶1379.content: - 3.12.1.2.1.2 Exception  1:  The  starting  point  of  a  Big  Metal  Mass  type  track  condition  shall  be evaluated  taking  into  account  the  max  safe  antenna  position,  the  end  of  the  profile taking into account the min safe antenna position.

¶1380.content: - 3.12.1.2.1.3 Exception 2: The end of the Powerless section and the end of the Station Platform shall be evaluated taking into account the min safe front end of the train.

¶1381.content: - 3.12.1.2.1.4 Exception 3: The start and end of a tunnel stopping area and of a sound horn track condition shall be evaluated taking into account the estimated front end of the train.

¶1382.content: - 3.12.1.3 The types of track conditions to be covered by this function are:

¶1383.content: -  Powerless  section,  lower  pantograph  (initial  state:  no  powerless  section,  i.e. pantograph not to be lowered)

¶1384.content: -  Powerless section, switch off main power switch (initial state: no powerless section, i.e. main power switch not to be switched off)

¶1385.content: -  Air tightness (initial state: no request for air tightness)

¶1386.content: -  Sound horn (initial state: no request for sound horn)

¶1387.content: -  Non stopping area (initial state: stopping permitted)

¶1388.content: -  Tunnel stopping area (initial state: no tunnel stopping area)

¶1389.content: -  Change of traction system, switch traction system on-board, used for train capable of handling several traction systems (initial state: no initial state - keep the current setting)

¶1390.content: -  Change of allowed current consumption, limit current consumed by the train, used to  adapt  the  maximum  current  consumption  of  the  train  to  the  maximum  current allowed by the trackside (initial state: no initial state - keep the current setting)

¶1391.content: -  Big  metal  masses,  ignore  onboard  integrity  check  alarms  of  balise  transmission. (initial state: alarms not ignored)

¶1392.content: -  Radio hole, stop supervision of the loss of safe radio connection (initial state: loss of safe radio connection supervised)

¶1393.content: -  Switch off regenerative brake (initial state: regenerative brake on)

¶1394.content: -  Switch off eddy current brake for service brake (initial state: eddy current brake on for service brake)

¶1395.content: -  Switch off eddy current brake for emergency brake (initial state: eddy current brake on for emergency brake)

¶1396.content: -  Switch off magnetic shoe brake (initial state: magnetic shoe brake on).

¶1397.content: -  Station  platform,  enable  passenger  doors  with  or  without  steps  according  to platform  location,  side  and  height  (initial  state:  no  platform,  i.e.  passenger  doors not enabled).

¶1398.content: - 3.12.1.3.1  Note: In case of regenerative brake switch off or magnetic shoe brake switch off, the deceleration  of  the  emergency  brake  might  be  affected  if  the  effect  of  these  brakes was included in the calculation of the deceleration value .

¶1399.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1400.content: - 3.12.1.3.2  Note: In case of eddy current brake switch off the deceleration of the service brake or emergency brake might be affected if the effect of these brakes was included in the calculation of the deceleration value .

¶1401.content: - 3.12.1.3.3  Note: in case of powerless section the deceleration of the service brake or emergency brake might be affected if the effect of a regenerative brake not independent from the presence of voltage in the catenary was included in the calculation of the deceleration value.

¶1403.content: - 3.12.1.5 The following actions shall be performed once a track condition has been received:

¶1404.content: - Indicate on DMI (see chapter 5, procedure 'Indication of track conditions'), except 'Station  platform',  'Change  of  allowed  current  consumption'  and  'Big  metal masses'.

¶1405.content: - Send information with the remaining distance to an ERTMS/ETCS external function (see chapter 5, procedure 'Generation of track conditions related information to an ERTMS/ETCS  external  function'),  with  the  exception  of  big  metal  mass  track condition, sound horn track condition, non stopping area, tunnel stopping area and supervision of  radio transmission which are handled inside the ERTMS/ETCS onboard equipment.

¶1406.content: - 3.12.1.5.1  Note: Whether some information shall be filtered (not shown to the driver or not sent to an ERTMS/ETCS external function) is outside the scope of ERTMS/ETCS.

¶1407.content: - 3.12.1.5.2  Note: The ERTMS/ETCS external function must be able to handle new track condition of the same type as previously received and covering the same distance.

¶1408.content: - 3.12.1.6 The  train  is  permitted  to  run  without  any  track  condition  information  given  from  the trackside. The initial state shall then be used by the on-board equipment.

¶1410.content: - 3.12.2.1 Route suitability data defines which values concerning loading gauge, traction system and axle load category a train must meet to be allowed to enter the route.

¶1411.content: - 3.12.2.2 It  shall  be  possible  for  trackside  to  send  route  suitability  data  as  location  data  when needed.

¶1412.content: - 3.12.2.3 On  reception  of  route  suitability  data,  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall compare it with the corresponding Train Data stored on-board. Unsuitability exists if:

¶1413.content: - The loading gauge profile of the train is not included in the list of loading gauges accepted by trackside

¶1414.content: - The  list  of  traction  systems  accepted  by  the  engine  does  not  include  the  one received from trackside

¶1415.content: -  The axle load category of the train is higher than the permitted one received from trackside

¶1416.content: - 3.12.2.4 If at least one unsuitability exists, the closest location corresponding to the unsuitability(ies)  shall  be  considered  as  both  the  EOA  and  SvL  (instead  of  the EOA/LOA and the  SvL  (if  any)  derived  from  the  MA),  with  no  Release  Speed.  The driver shall be informed about all unsuitabilities.

¶1421.content: - 3.12.2.8 If, for any reasons, the train overpasses the location of the first route suitability where incompatibility occurs, it shall be tripped.

¶1422.content: - 3.12.2.9 The Train Data concerning route suitability is part of the Train Data sent to the RBC.

¶1423.content: - 3.12.2.9.1  Note: This allows for route suitability supervision to be used in systems external to the ERTMS/ETCS system.

¶1424.content: - 3.12.2.10 The train is permitted to run without any route suitability data given from the track. No default values shall be used or supervised by the on-board equipment, i.e. the initial state is that no restrictions related to route suitability exists.

¶1427.content: - 3.12.3.1.1  It  shall  be  possible  to  transmit  information  to  be  displayed  to  the  driver  from  the trackside to the on-board equipment in the form of text messages.

¶1428.content: - 3.12.3.1.2  Text messages shall always be supplemented by conditions on when and where they are to be displayed, and whether any acknowledgement is requested from the driver. These parameters shall be transmitted individually for each message.

¶1429.content: - 3.12.3.1.3  Text messages and the supplementary information shall always be transmitted in one message.

¶1430.content: - 3.12.3.1.4  It shall be possible to send the text to be displayed in plain text or to send a number selecting a fixed message.

¶1431.content: - 3.12.3.1.4.1 Note: In case of plain text messages the trackside selects the language in which the message is displayed.

¶1436.content: - 3.12.3.1.9  The following data shall be included in a text message:

¶1437.content: -  Class of message (auxiliary or important information)

¶1438.content: -  Plain text message or fixed message number

¶1441.content: -  If driver acknowledgement is requested or not

¶1442.content: - 3.12.3.1.10  The  appearance  of  a  message  shall  depend  on  the  class  and  on  whether  a  driver acknowledgement is requested.

¶1443.content: - 3.12.3.1.11  It shall be possible for trackside to send a text message with a request to report driver acknowledgement, if any, to an RBC.

¶1445.content: - 3.12.3.3.1  Fixed text messages shall be stored on-board in all languages that can be selected by the driver.

¶1451.content: - 3.12.3.4.1  It shall be possible to specify individual events for start/end condition of indication.

¶1452.content: - 3.12.3.4.2  The following events can be used to define the start condition:

¶1454.content: -  Mode (start display as soon as in mode)

¶1455.content: -  Level (start display as soon as in level)

¶1456.content: - 3.12.3.4.3  The following events can be used to define the end condition:

¶1460.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1462.content: - 3.12.3.4.3.1 It  shall be possible to define whether one or all of the events used from the list in 3.12.3.4.2/3.12.3.4.3 have  to  be  fulfilled to define  the start/end condition.  This definition shall apply to both start and end conditions checked by the on-board.

¶1463.content: - 3.12.3.4.3.2 In  case  a  confirmation  of  the  text  message  is  requested,  it  shall  be  possible  to define whether the driver acknowledgement is considered:

¶1464.content: - As  always  ending  the  text  display,  regardless  of  the  end  condition  defined  in 3.12.3.4.3.1

¶1465.content: - As  a  necessary  condition  to  end  the  text  display,  in  addition  to  the  end  condition defined in 3.12.3.4.3.1.

¶1466.content: - 3.12.3.4.4  The  end  condition  shall  be  evaluated  as  soon  as  the  start  condition  is  fulfilled.  No display shall take place if the end condition is immediately fulfilled.

¶1467.content: - 3.12.3.4.5  Once the text message is displayed and the end condition is fulfilled, the start condition shall not be re-evaluated.

¶1468.content: - 3.12.3.4.6  When the end event "location" is used, the length on which the text is displayed shall refer to the location used for the start condition, independently from other start events.

¶1469.content: - 3.12.3.4.7  In case a confirmation of the text message is requested, it shall be possible to define whether the service brake or emergency brake application shall be commanded if the driver does not acknowledge before the end condition is fulfilled.

¶1470.content: - 3.12.3.4.7.1 If  the  driver  does  not  acknowledge  before  the  end  condition  is  fulfilled,  the  text message shall remain displayed until acknowledged by driver.

¶1471.content: - 3.12.3.4.7.2 If  the  driver  acknowledges  before  the  end  condition  is  fulfilled,  the  on-board equipment shall consider the driver acknowledgement as requested by trackside (see 3.12.3.4.3.2).

¶1473.content: # 3.12.3.5 Report of driver acknowledgement to RBC

¶1474.content: - 3.12.3.5.1  If trackside requests a report of driver acknowledgement, then it shall include:

¶1476.content: -  the identity of the RBC to which the driver acknowledgement report is to be sent.

¶1477.content: - 3.12.3.5.2  When  the  driver  has  acknowledged  a  text  message  with  a  request  to  report  driver acknowledgement,  the  driver  acknowledgement  report,  including  the  text  message identifier, shall be sent to the RBC referenced in the request.

¶1478.content: - 3.12.3.5.3  A  new  text  message  with  request  for  report  of  driver  acknowledgement  shall  be rejected  by  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  if  it  has  the  same  text  message

¶1479.content: identifier  as  a  previously  received  text  message,  which  the  driver  has  not  yet acknowledged.

¶1481.content: - 3.12.4.1 It shall be possible for trackside to send a Mode Profile. The Mode Profile can request On Sight mode, Limited Supervision mode and Shunting mode.

¶1482.content: - 3.12.4.2 For  OS  and  LS  mode  the  mode  profile  defines  the  entry  and  the  length  of  the  On Sight/Limited Supervision area. For SH mode the mode profile only defines the entry location to SH mode, any length given shall be ignored by the on-board.

¶1483.content: - 3.12.4.3 On reception of a new MA (with or without Mode Profile) the on-board equipment shall delete the currently supervised Mode Profile.

¶1484.content: - 3.12.4.3.1  Exception: When receiving a new MA by infill, any currently supervised Mode Profile shall be deleted only beyond the reference location of the infill information.

¶1485.content: - 3.12.4.4 In  case  the  mode  profile  information  for  shunting  is  overwritten  by  a  new  shunting profile, before the on-board equipment switches to SH mode, a previous list of balise groups for SH area shall be deleted or replaced by a new list of balise groups for SH area.

¶1486.content: - 3.12.4.5 The beginning of the Mode Profile relates to the max safe front end of the train.

¶1487.content: - 3.12.4.6 The end of the mode profile relates to the min safe front end of the train.

¶1489.content: - 3.12.5.1 It  shall  be  possible  for  trackside  to  inform  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment about the conditions under which a Level Crossing (LX) must be passed.

¶1490.content: - 3.12.5.2 Each Level Crossing shall have an identity, so that all LX information is independent of each other. This means that an individual LX information cannot affect, nor be affected by, any other individual LX information.

¶1491.content: - 3.12.5.3 If the ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment receives a new LX information with the same identity  as  an  already  received  LX  information,  the  new  LX  information shall  replace the previous one.

¶1492.content: - 3.12.5.4 Level Crossing information shall be given as profile data, corresponding to the LX start location and the length of the LX area.

¶1493.content: - 3.12.5.5 Level Crossing information shall indicate whether the LX is protected or not.

¶1494.content: - 3.12.5.6 In case the LX is not protected, ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment shall be informed:

¶1495.content: - at which speed the LX is allowed to be passed

¶1496.content: - whether the stopping of the train in rear of the LX start location is required or not

¶1497.content: - 3.12.5.7 In case stopping in rear of the non protected LX is required, a stopping area in rear of the LX start location shall be defined.

¶1500.content: - 3.13.1.1 The speed and distance monitoring is the supervision of the speed of the train versus its  position,  in  order  to  assure  that  the  train  remains  within  the  given  speed  and distance limits.

¶1501.content: - 3.13.1.1.1  Note: The speed and distance monitoring of the on-board can only assure this when the following necessary conditions are fulfilled:

¶1502.content: -  Brake system of the train functions as specified

¶1503.content: -  wheel/rail adhesion is sufficient for the required safe deceleration

¶1504.content: -  Brake characteristics (and other Train related inputs) are correctly entered into the on-board

¶1505.content: - 3.13.1.2 Note: The ERTMS/ETCS on-board equipment triggers brake commands and revokes them,  it  may  also  receive  status  information  if  the  brakes  are  applied  or  released. However, it cannot be made responsible if brake control circuits outside the equipment fail.  Also the way the brakes are released by the driver after a revocation of a brake command is an implementation issue.

¶1506.content: - 3.13.1.3 Figure  28  gives  an  overview  of  the  main  elements  contributing  to  the  speed  and distance monitoring. These elements (inputs, functions and outputs) are detailed in the following chapters.

¶1507.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1508.content: Figure 28: Speed and distance monitoring overview

¶1509.content: - 3.13.1.4 Throughout  the  following  sections,  all  the  distances  marked  with  'd'  (lower  case), which are referred in parameters, formulas and figures, are counted from the current reference location of the on-board equipment (e.g. the LRBG).

¶1510.content: # 3.13.2 Inputs for speed and distance monitoring

¶1512.content: - 3.13.2.1.1  The traction / braking models, the brake position / brake percentage are used for the definition of the kinematic behaviour of the train after a service brake command or an emergency brake command has been initiated.

¶1513.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1514.content: - 3.13.2.1.2  However, railway brakes have a statistical behaviour and braking distances vary within the typical distribution for a given condition. Correction factors are therefore incorporated for the speed and distance monitoring.

¶1515.content: - 3.13.2.1.3  The  correction  factors  will  allow  obtaining,  from  the  nominal  emergency  braking performance  of  the  train,  the  minimum  emergency  braking  performances  that  are required for reference conditions set by trackside.

¶1518.content: - 3.13.2.2.1.1 The train related inputs to be considered for the speed and distance monitoring are:

¶1520.content: - Braking models (brake build up time and speed dependent deceleration) or brake percentage

¶1528.content: -  Fixed values related to speed and distance monitoring

¶1529.content: -   Train related speed restriction (i.e. the maximum train speed)

¶1530.content: - 3.13.2.2.1.2 These train related inputs are acquired as Train Data (see 3.18.3.2 items b) c) and d)), except:

¶1531.content: -  the configuration of the special brakes, service brake and traction cut-off interfaces which are not affected by the Train Data acquisition,

¶1532.content: -  the service brake application and the special brakes statuses which are continuously acquired on the Train Interface,

¶1534.content: - 3.13.2.2.1.3 The  speed  and  distance  monitoring  shall  use  braking  models  acquired  as  Train Data, unless the brake percentage is acquired as Train Data and the conversion model is applicable (see 3.13.3.2 for its validity limits).

¶1536.content: - 3.13.2.2.2.1 The traction model shall be given as a step function as indicated in  Figure 29. It shall describe the time delay T_traction_cut_off from the traction cut-off command by

¶1537.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1538.content: the  on-board  (t0)  to  the  moment  the  acceleration  due  to  traction  (A_traction)  is guaranteed  to  be  zero  (t1).  The  estimated  acceleration  value  of  the  train  shall  be considered during this time delay.

¶1540.content: - 3.13.2.2.2.2 Note:  The  current  value  of  A_traction  is  not  known  directly  by  the  on-board.  It  is implicitly  known  as  a  contribution  to  the  estimated  acceleration,  together  with  the acceleration due to gradient.

¶1543.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.1 The deceleration due to braking shall be given as a step function of the speed.

¶1544.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.2 It  shall  be  possible  to  define  up  to  seven  steps  for  each  speed  dependent deceleration model.

¶1545.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.3 Note: An example with 4 steps is given in  Figure 30. A_brake(V) is calculated as follows:

¶1547.content: -  A_brake = AD_1  when V1 < speed ≤ V2

¶1548.content: -  A_brake = AD_2  when V2 < speed ≤ V3

¶1550.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1552.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.4 The last step of A_brake(V) shall by definition be considered as open ended, i.e. it has no upper speed limit.

¶1553.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.5 The model shall be applicable only after full build up of the braking effort (see a_full in Figure 31)

¶1554.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.6 The model shall be used for the emergency brake nominal deceleration (A_brake_emergency(V)), for the full service brake deceleration (A_brake_service(V)) and for the normal service brake deceleration (A_brake_normal_service(V)).

¶1555.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.7 It  shall  be  possible  to  define  individual  speed  dependent  deceleration  models  of A_brake_emergency(V)  and  A_brake_service(V)  for  each  combination  of  use  of regenerative brake, eddy current brake and magnetic shoe brake.

¶1556.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.8 Note: Individual deceleration models may be equal, thereby avoiding the influence of  a  specific  brake  on  A_brake_emergency(V)  or  A_brake_service(V).  However,  the choice to take into account or not the contribution of a specific brake for A_brake_emergency(V) or A_brake_service(V) is only rolling stock dependent, not an ETCS implementation issue.

¶1557.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.9 It shall be possible to define up to two sets of three models of A_brake_normal_service(V):

¶1558.content: - one set applicable when the brake position is in 'Freight train in G'

¶1559.content: - one set applicable when the brake position is in 'Passenger train in P' or 'Freight train in P'

¶1560.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.10 A  set  of  A_brake_normal_service(V)  shall  be  defined  as  a  function  of  the  full service brake deceleration at zero speed, A_brake_service(V=0):

¶1567.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.1.11 Note: the two pivot values A_SB01 and A_SB12 are part of the A_brake_normal_service model, i.e. they are train related input data for the speed and distance monitoring function.

¶1569.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1570.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.1 The deceleration A_brake is not available immediately after the on-board commands the brake. There is a time lag between brake command and the start of the brake force build-up. There is also time needed to build up the full brake force.

¶1571.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.2 The model for the brake build up time shall be given as a step function as explained in Figure 31.

¶1572.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.3 In Figure 31, the following time intervals are defined:

¶1573.content: - T_brake_react (t0…t1) is the interval between the command of the brake by the onboard and the moment the brake force starts to build up.

¶1574.content: - T_brake_increase (t1...t2) is the interval in which the brake force increases from the zero to the moment when 95% of full brake power is reached.

¶1575.content: -  T_brake_build_up (t0...t3) is the equivalent brake build up time.

¶1576.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.4 The equivalent brake build up time (T_brake_build_up) is defined as T_brake_build_up = T_brake_react + 0.5*T_brake_increase.

¶1577.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.5 This model for T_brake_build_up shall be used for the emergency brake (T_brake_emergency) and for the full service brake (T_brake_service).

¶1578.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.6 Note: The equivalent brake build up time is a safe approximation. In the beginning of the build-up time the model assumes a smaller deceleration, in the later part this is compensated by a higher deceleration.

¶1579.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.7 Note: T_brake_react and T_brake_increase are indicated in Figure 31 for completeness reasons; only T_brake_build_up is to be considered as an input for the speed and distance monitoring function.

¶1580.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.8 It shall be possible to define individual  values  of  T_brake_emergency  and T_brake_service  for  each  combination  of  use  of  regenerative  brake,  eddy  current brake, magnetic shoe brake and Ep brake.

¶1581.content: Figure 31: Brake Build Up Time Model

¶1582.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1583.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.9 Note: Individual values of T_brake_emergency and T_brake_service may be equal, thereby  avoiding  the  influence  of  a  specific  brake.  However,  the  choice  to  take  into account  or  not  the  contribution  of  a  specific  brake  for  T_brake_emergency  and T_brake_service is only rolling stock dependent, not an ETCS implementation issue.

¶1584.content: - 3.13.2.2.3.2.10 Note: In general, T_brake_emergency and T_brake_service are determined by the  pneumatic  brake  therefore  avoiding  to  take  into  account  of  the  influence  of  the regenerative  brake,  eddy  current  brake  or  magnetic  shoe  brake.  However,  if  the Electro-pneumatic brake system is used, it is possible that T_brake_emergency and T_brake_service are determined by another special brake.

¶1586.content: - 3.13.2.2.4.1 The brake position shall be set to one of the following three values:

¶1590.content: - 3.13.2.2.4.2 Note: The brake position defines the behaviour of the brake for specific train types.

¶1592.content: - 3.13.2.2.5.1 If  the  brake  percentage  is  captured  as  Train  Data  and  the  conversion  model  is applicable (see 3.13.3.2), they are used to derive A_brake_emergency(V), A_brake_service(V), T_brake_emergency and T_brake_service.

¶1593.content: - 3.13.2.2.5.2 Note: the conversion model has been designed assuming that all the provisions laid down in the UIC leaflet 544-1 6 th  edition, with the exception of sections 9.1.2, 9.2.2 and 9.3.3, apply for the acquired brake percentage.

¶1595.content: - 3.13.2.2.6.1 For  each  special  brake  (regenerative  brake,  eddy  current  brake,  magnetic  shoe brake and electro-pneumatic brake), the on-board shall be configured to define one of the following possibilities marked with an 'X' in Table 3

¶1596.content: - 3.13.2.2.6.2 When an interface exists with the regenerative brake, eddy current brake, magnetic shoe brake system and/or the Ep brake on-board system and depending whether their status affects the concerned brake parameter(s), the speed and distance monitoring shall  take  into  account  their  status  'active'  or  'not  active'  to  select  the  appropriate brake parameter(s) captured as Train Data, according to Table 4:

¶1597.content:
|               |                     | configuration possibilities   | configuration possibilities                                        | configuration possibilities                                      | configuration possibilities                                                 |
|---------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|               |                     | No interface exists           | Interface exists and status affects the emergency brake model only | Interface exists and status affects the service brake model only | Interface exists and status affects both emergency and service brake models |
| Special brake | regenerative brake  | x                             | x                                                                  | x                                                                | x                                                                           |
| Special brake | eddy current brake  | x                             | x                                                                  | x                                                                | x                                                                           |
| Special brake | magnetic shoe brake | x                             | x                                                                  |                                                                  |                                                                             |
| Special brake | Ep brake            | x                             |                                                                    | x                                                                | x                                                                           |

¶1598.content: Table 3: On-board Configuration in relation to special brakes

¶1599.content:
|                 |                      | When interface exists and if status affects the brake parameter, selection of brake parameter according to status of:   | When interface exists and if status affects the brake parameter, selection of brake parameter according to status of:   | When interface exists and if status affects the brake parameter, selection of brake parameter according to status of:   | When interface exists and if status affects the brake parameter, selection of brake parameter according to status of:   |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|                 |                      | regenerative brake                                                                                                      | eddy current brake                                                                                                      | magnetic shoe brake                                                                                                     | Ep brake                                                                                                                |
| Brake parameter | A_brake_emergency(V) | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       |                                                                                                                         |
| Brake parameter | T_brake_emergency    | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       |
| Brake parameter | A_brake_service(V)   | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       |                                                                                                                         |                                                                                                                         |
| Brake parameter | T_brake_service      | x                                                                                                                       | x                                                                                                                       |                                                                                                                         | x                                                                                                                       |

¶1600.content: Table 4: Selection of brake parameters according to status of special brakes

¶1601.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1602.content: - 3.13.2.2.6.3 When the brake percentage is captured as Train Data and the conversion model is applicable, A_brake_emergency(V), T_brake_emergency and A_brake_service(V) shall  not  be  influenced  by  the  status  of  a  special  brake.  However,  the  conversion model offers the possibility that T_brake_service can be affected by the status of the regenerative brake, eddy current brake or Ep brake (see A.3.9).

¶1603.content: - 3.13.2.2.6.4 The on-board equipment shall be configured to define whether it is allowed to take into account the contribution of a special/additional brake, which is independent from wheel/rail adhesion, for the selection of the maximum emergency brake deceleration under reduced adhesion conditions (see 3.13.6.2.1.6).

¶1604.content: - 3.13.2.2.6.5 Note: the choice to set to 'allowed' the contribution of such special/additional brake in the selection of the maximum emergency braking effort, is rolling stock dependent.

¶1605.content: - 3.13.2.2.6.6 If  it  is  allowed  to  take  into  account  the  contribution  of  a  special/additional  brake, which  is  independent  from  wheel/rail  adhesion,  the  speed  and  distance  monitoring function shall take into account the status 'active' or 'not active' of the special/additional  brake  to  select  the  appropriate  National  Value  under  reduced adhesion conditions (see 3.13.2.3.7.7).

¶1607.content: - 3.13.2.2.7.1 The on-board shall be configured to define whether the service brake command is implemented or not, i.e. whether a service brake interface is implemented to command a full service brake effort.

¶1608.content: - 3.13.2.2.7.2 The on-board shall be configured to define whether the service brake feedback is implemented or not, i.e. whether it is able to acquire from the service brake interface the information that the service brake is currently applied.

¶1609.content: - 3.13.2.2.7.3 If the service brake feedback is implemented and if not inhibited by National Value, the  speed  and  distance  monitoring  function  shall  take  into  account  either  the  main brake pipe pressure or the brake cylinder pressure to adjust in real time the expected brake build up time (see 3.13.9.3.3.4 and Appendix A.3.10).

¶1611.content: - 3.13.2.2.8.1 The on-board shall be configured to define whether the traction cut-off command is implemented, i.e. whether the interface to the traction system is implemented or not.

¶1613.content: # 3.13.2.2.9.1 Correction factors for the emergency deceleration

¶1614.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.1.1 If  the  braking  models  are  captured  as  Train  Data,  rolling  stock  correction  factors shall be defined in the ETCS on-board equipment. If the brake percentage is captured as Train Data and the conversion model is used (see 3.13.3.2 for its validity limits), no rolling stock correction factor shall apply.

¶1615.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1616.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.1.2 For each defined individual speed dependent deceleration model of A_brake_emergency(V) (i.e. corresponding to each combination of use of regenerative brake,  eddy  current  brake  and  magnetic  shoe  brake),  one  set  of  rolling  stock correction factors Kdry_rst(V, EBCL) and Kwet_rst(V) shall be defined in the on-board equipment.

¶1617.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.1.3 For  a  given  confidence  level  on  emergency  brake  safe  deceleration  (EBCL),  the rolling  stock correction factor Kdry_rst(V) shall be given as a step function of speed, with the same steps as the ones of A_brake_emergency(V).

¶1618.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.1.4 The confidence  level  on  emergency  brake  safe  deceleration  represents  the probability of the following individual  event:  the  rolling  stock  emergency  brake subsystem of the train does ensure a deceleration at least equal to A_brake_emergency(V) * Kdry_rst(V), when the emergency brake is commanded on dry rails.

¶1619.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.1.5 The rolling stock correction factor Kwet_rst(V) shall be given as a step function of speed, with the same steps as the ones of A_brake_emergency(V). It represents the loss  of  deceleration  with  regards  to  emergency  braking  on  dry  rails,  when  the emergency  brake  is  commanded  on  wet  rails,  according  to  wheel/rail  adhesion reference conditions.

¶1620.content: # 3.13.2.2.9.2 Correction factor for gradient on normal service deceleration

¶1621.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.2.1 The speed dependent correction factors for gradient on the normal service brake, Kn+(V) and Kn-(V), shall be given as step functions in the range from 0 to 10 m/s2.

¶1622.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.2.2 It shall be possible to define up to five steps for Kn+(V) and for Kn-(V), respectively.

¶1623.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.2.3 Note: An example with 4 steps is given in Figure 32. Kn is calculated as follows:

¶1625.content: -  Kn = Kn_1 when V1 < speed ≤ V2

¶1626.content: -  Kn = Kn_2 when V2 < speed ≤ V3

¶1628.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1629.content: Figure 32  Speed dependent correction factor for normal service brake (Kn)

¶1630.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.2.4 Kn+(V) shall be applicable for positive gradients.

¶1631.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.2.5 Kn-(V) shall be applicable for negative gradients.

¶1632.content: - 3.13.2.2.9.2.6 The  last  step  of  the  Kn+(V)  or  Kn-(V)  shall  by  definition  be  considered  as  open ended, i.e. it has no upper speed limit.

¶1634.content: - 3.13.2.2.10.1  It shall be possible to define the nominal rotating mass to be used for compensating the gradient, instead of the two related fixed values defined in A.3.1.

¶1636.content: - 3.13.2.2.11.1  The speed and distance monitoring shall take into account the train length acquired as part of Train Data (see section 3.18.3).

¶1638.content: - 3.13.2.2.12.1  The speed and distance monitoring shall take into account the fixed values defined in A.3.1 that are related to speed and distance monitoring.

¶1640.content: - 3.13.2.2.13.1  The  speed  and  distance  monitoring  shall  take  into  account  the  maximum  train speed defined as part of Train Data (see section 3.18.3).

¶1643.content: - 3.13.2.3.1.1 The trackside related inputs to be considered for the speed and distance monitoring are:

¶1646.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1647.content: -  Track conditions related to brake inhibition

¶1648.content: - Track conditions related to powerless section

¶1650.content: -  Specific speed and distance limits (e.g. EOA/SvL)

¶1653.content: - 3.13.2.3.2.1 The  speed  and  distance  monitoring  shall  take  into  account  the  trackside  related speed restrictions composed of all speed restrictions mentioned in 3.11.2 except the maximum train speed.

¶1655.content: - 3.13.2.3.3.1 The speed and distance monitoring shall take into account the gradient profile and the default gradient for TSR (see section 3.11.12).

¶1657.content: - 3.13.2.3.4.1 The speed and distance monitoring shall take into  account the  following types of track  condition  received  from  trackside  (see  section  3.12.1):  powerless  section, inhibition of regenerative brake, eddy current brake and magnetic shoe brake.

¶1659.content: - 3.13.2.3.5.1 The  speed  and  distance  monitoring  shall  take  into  account  the  track  reduced adhesion received from trackside or selected by the driver (see section 3.18.4.6).

¶1661.content: - 3.13.2.3.6.1 The speed and distance monitoring shall take into account the following limits:

¶1662.content: - the Limit of Authority (LOA), the End of Authority (EOA), the Supervised Location (SvL) and its associated release speed, if any.

¶1663.content: - the maximum permitted distance to run in Staff Responsible

¶1664.content: # 3.13.2.3.7  National Values for speed and distance monitoring

¶1665.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.1 It  shall  be  possible  by  means of a National Value to inhibit the use of the service brake command in target speed monitoring.

¶1666.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.2 It  shall  be  possible  to  state  by  means of a National Value whether an emergency brake command has to be revoked, both in ceiling speed and target speed monitoring, when:

¶1667.content: - the Permitted Speed supervision limit is no longer exceeded, or

¶1669.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.3 It  shall  be  possible  by  means  of  a  National  Value  to  inhibit  the  guidance  curve (GUI).

¶1670.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.4 It  shall  be  possible  by  means  of  a  National  Value  to  inhibit  the  service  brake feedback function.

¶1671.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.5 It  shall  be  possible  by  means  of  National  Values  to  indicate  to  the  on-board equipment the required confidence level  on  the  emergency  brake safe deceleration, when the emergency brake is commanded on dry rails (see 3.13.2.2.9.1.4).

¶1672.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.6 It  shall  be  possible  by  means  of  a  National  Value  to  indicate  to  the  on-board equipment the available wheel/rail adhesion, weighted between the wheel/rail adhesion for dry rails and the wheel/rail adhesion for wet rails according to reference conditions.

¶1673.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.7 In order to adapt to the train behaviour under reduced adhesion conditions, it shall be possible by means of National Values either to limit to a maximum value the speed dependent  deceleration for the emergency  brake  when  the  reduced  adhesion conditions  are  known  to  ETCS  (see  3.13.2.3.5)  or  to  request  supplementary  DMI information assisting further the driver in ceiling speed monitoring. Three values shall be applicable for a given combination of the brake position and of the type of brakes:

¶1674.content: - the first value shall be used for 'Passenger train in P' with special/additional brakes independent from wheel/rail adhesion;

¶1675.content: - the second value shall be used for 'Passenger train in P' without special/additional brakes independent from wheel/rail adhesion;

¶1676.content: -  the third value shall be used for 'Freight train in P' or 'Freight train in G'.

¶1677.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.8 It shall be possible by means of a National Value to specify a release speed.

¶1678.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.9 It shall be possible by means of a National Value to inhibit the compensation of the speed measurement inaccuracy.

¶1679.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.10  It  shall  be  possible  by  means  of  National  Values  to  define  integrated  correction factors,  namely  Kv_int(V),  Kr_int(l)  and  Kt_int.  The  integrated  correction  factors  only apply to the on-board equipment when the conversion model is used.

¶1680.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11  The speed dependent correction factor, Kv_int(V), shall be given as a step function.

¶1681.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.1 It shall be possible to define up to five steps for Kv_int(V).

¶1682.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.2 Note:  An  example  with  4  steps  is  given  in  Figure  33.  Kv_int  is  calculated  as follows:

¶1684.content: -  Kv_int = Kv_int_1 when V1 < speed ≤ V2

¶1685.content: -  Kv_int = Kv_int_2 when V2 < speed ≤ V3

¶1687.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1688.content: Figure 33  Speed dependent correction factor Kv_int

¶1689.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.3 It shall be possible to define up to 2 sets of Kv_int with separate speed limits V1, V2, .. for each set. The sets of Kv_int relate to the following train types:

¶1692.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.3.1 Note: Different sets of Kv_int are needed for different types of trains in order to compensate  the  absence  of  the  rolling  stock  related  correction  factors  when  the conversion model is used.

¶1693.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.4 The set of Kv_int for conventional passenger trains shall be divided into two sub sets Kv_int_x_a and Kv_int_x_b, with identical speed limits V1, V2, ....

¶1694.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.5 Subset Kv_int_x_a shall be applicable for maximum emergency brake deceleration lower or equal to a deceleration limit, defined as a National Value.

¶1695.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.11.6 Subset Kv_int_x_b shall be applicable for maximum emergency brake deceleration greater or equal to a deceleration limit, defined as a National Value.

¶1696.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.12  The  train  length  dependent  correction  factor,  Kr_int(l),  shall  be  given  as  a  step function.

¶1697.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.12.1 It shall be possible to define up to five steps for Kr_int(l).

¶1698.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.12.2 Note:  An  example  with  4  steps  is  given  in  Figure  34.  Kr_int  is  calculated  as follows:

¶1699.content: -  Kr_int = Kr_int_0 when 0 ≤ train length ≤ L1

¶1700.content: -  Kr_int = Kr_int_1 when L1 < train length ≤ L2

¶1701.content: -  Kr_int = Kr_int_2 when L2 < train length ≤ L3

¶1702.content: -  Kr_int = Kr_int_3 when L3 < train length

¶1703.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1704.content: Figure 34  Train length dependent correction factor Kr_int

¶1705.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.13  The last step of the Kv_int(V) and Kr_int(l) shall by definition be considered as open ended, i.e. it has no upper speed and train length limit, respectively.

¶1706.content: - 3.13.2.3.7.14  The correction factor for brake build up time (Kt_int) shall be a single parameter.

¶1709.content: - 3.13.3.1.1  For trains with variable composition (loco hauled trains), the brake characteristics can vary together with the composition of the train. In this case, it is not convenient to preprogram  the  brake  parameters  necessary  to  calculate  the  braking  curves.  The  only practical way to obtain the correct values for the current train composition is to include them into the data entry process by the driver. However, it cannot be expected from the driver to know deceleration values and brake build up times. Conversion models are therefore defined to convert the parameters  entered  by  the  driver (brake percentage and brake position) into the parameters of the corresponding brake model.

¶1710.content: - 3.13.3.1.2  Note: The process for defining the input parameters for the conversion model (brake percentage and brake position) is outside the scope of the  ERTMS/ETCS specifications.

¶1712.content: - 3.13.3.2.1  The  conversion  models  shall  be  used  by  the  on-board  equipment  if  the  brake percentage  is  acquired  as  part  of  Train  Data,  and  if  the  maximum  train  speed,  the brake percentage and the train length are all within the following validity limits of the conversion models:

¶1713.content: - 0 ≤ V ≤ 200, where V is the maximum train speed in km/h

¶1714.content: - 30 ≤ λ ≤ 250, where λ is the brake percentage in %

¶1715.content: -  0 ≤ L ≤ Lmax, where L is the train length in m and where Lmax = 900 m if the brake position is 'Passenger train in P' or Lmax = 1500 m if the brake position is 'Freight train in P' or 'Freight train in G'

¶1716.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1717.content: - 3.13.3.2.1.1 Note: The overspeed above the maximum train speed which may occur due to the ceiling speed margins is taken into account in the definition of the conversion model.

¶1718.content: - 3.13.3.2.2  For trains not fitting into at least one of those validity limits, it is still possible to acquire the  brake  percentage  as  Train  Data,  but  the  conversion  models  are  not  applicable, which  means  that  braking  models  (i.e.  pre-programmed  deceleration  profiles  and brake build up times) shall be used by the speed and distance monitoring function.

¶1721.content: - 3.13.3.3.1.1 The  input  for  the  model  shall  be  the  brake  percentage  of  the  train  as  defined  in 3.13.2.2.5.

¶1723.content: - 3.13.3.3.2.1 The basic deceleration A_basic(V) shall be given as a step function of the speed using the algorithm defined in Appendix A.3.7.

¶1725.content: - 3.13.3.3.3.1 The output of  the  brake  percentage  conversion  model shall  consist  of  two  speed dependent  deceleration  brake  models,  A_brake_emergency(V)  for  the  emergency brake and A_brake_service(V) for the service brake.

¶1728.content: - 3.13.3.4.1.1 The input for the model shall consist of the brake position of the train as defined in 3.13.2.2.4, the train length and the target speed.

¶1729.content: # 3.13.3.4.2  Calculation of the emergency brake equivalent time

¶1730.content: - 3.13.3.4.2.1 The equivalent brake build up time for the emergency brake shall be determined as specified in Appendix A.3.8.

¶1731.content: # 3.13.3.4.3  Calculation of the full service brake equivalent time

¶1732.content: - 3.13.3.4.3.1 The equivalent brake build up time for the full service brake shall be determined as specified in Appendix A.3.9.

¶1734.content: - 3.13.3.4.4.1 The outputs of the brake position conversion model shall consist of:

¶1735.content: - two values of the equivalent brake build up time to be used when the target speed (V_target) is equal to zero, one value for the emergency brake and one for the full service brake:

¶1736.content: T_brake_emergency_cm0 as defined for emergency brake in A.3.8

¶1737.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1738.content: T_brake_service_cm0 as defined for service brake in A.3.9

¶1739.content: - two values of the equivalent brake build up time to be used when the target speed (V_target) is different from zero, one value for the emergency brake and one for the full service brake:

¶1740.content: T_brake_emergency_cmt as defined for emergency brake in A.3.8

¶1741.content: T_brake_service_cmt as defined for service brake in A.3.9

¶1744.content: - 3.13.4.1.1  The elements of the gradient profile given from trackside shall be compensated:

¶1745.content: - in location according to the train length as defined in 3.13.4.2

¶1746.content: - in value according to the rotating mass as defined in 3.13.4.3 in order to derive the corresponding acceleration/deceleration.

¶1748.content: Figure 35: Compensation on the gradient profile

¶1749.content: - 3.13.4.1.2  The default gradient for TSR shall be compensated in value according to the rotating mass as defined in 3.13.4.3.

¶1750.content: - 3.13.4.1.3  For  all  locations  not  covered  by  the  gradient  profile,  the  on-board  shall  consider  the gradient value as:

¶1751.content: - the default gradient for TSR, if available and if the concerned target is due to a TSR

¶1754.content: - 3.13.4.2.1  Assuming that a fictive train front  end  would  be at any location between the current (actual) train front end location and the SvL, the acceleration due to the gradient shall be determined using the lowest (taking the sign into account) gradient value given by the gradient profile between the location of the fictive train front end and the location of the fictive train rear end (see Figure 35).

¶1756.content: - 3.13.4.3.1  The influence of gradients shall be compensated for the rotating mass of the train (see Figure 35).

¶1757.content: - 3.13.4.3.1.1 Note: Since the rotating mass works like a flywheel (rotating inertia), the effect of the gradient is reduced. Assume for instance a (theoretical) train without any rotating mass,  not  braking,  on  a  downhill  gradient  from  height  1  to  height  2.  All  the  energy added to the train when it goes from H1 to H2 is converted into linear forward motion. This can be observed as an acceleration due to the gradient. Now assume the same train with part of the weight rotating. If this train travels the same distance from H1 to H2, the same amount of energy is added to the train. But now a part of that energy is converted  into  rotational  motion  and  only  the  remaining  part  is  converted  into  linear forward motion. The latter can be observed as an acceleration which is less than for the train without rotating mass.

¶1758.content: - 3.13.4.3.1.2 Note: For the influence of the rotating mass on the deceleration due to the brake, it is already taken into account in the values for the brake parameters.

¶1759.content: - 3.13.4.3.2  The following formulas shall be used:

¶1768.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1769.content: g = 9.81 m/s 2  - acceleration of gravity in m/s 2

¶1771.content: M_rotating_nom = nominal rotating mass (part of train data)  as a percentage of the total train weight

¶1772.content: M_rotating_max = maximum possible rotating mass (see A.3.1) as a percentage of the total train weight

¶1773.content: M_rotating_min = minimum possible rotating mass (see A.3.1) as a percentage of the total train weight

¶1774.content: # 3.13.5 Determination of locations without special brake contribution and with reduced adhesion conditions

¶1775.content: - 3.13.5.1 As  long  as  it  uses  a  track  condition  profile  given  by  trackside,  the  on-board  shall consider  locations  without  special  brake  contribution  over  a  distance  going  from  the start location of the profile to the foot of the deceleration curve (EBD, SBD or GUI, see sections 3.13.8.3, 3.13.8.4 and 3.13.8.5).

¶1776.content: - 3.13.5.2 If the status of a special brake is 'not active', all locations shall be considered without the contribution of this special brake.

¶1777.content: - 3.13.5.2.1  Note: in such case, a track condition profile implying the inhibition of this special brake will have no effect.

¶1778.content: - 3.13.5.3 From the adhesion profile given by trackside, the on-board shall consider locations with reduced adhesion conditions over a distance going from the start location of the profile to the location derived by adding the train length to the end location of the profile.

¶1779.content: - 3.13.5.4 When  slippery  rail  is  selected  by  the  driver,  all  locations  shall  be  considered  with reduced adhesion conditions.

¶1780.content: - 3.13.5.5 The  speed  and  distance monitoring shall use, as resulting  reduced  adhesion conditions, the most restrictive value of the adhesion conditions selected by the driver and the adhesion conditions calculated from the trackside profile.

¶1781.content: # 3.13.6 Calculation of the deceleration and brake build up time

¶1783.content: - 3.13.6.1.1  This chapter describes how the safe emergency brake, the expected and the normal service  brake  decelerations  and  the  time  intervals  due  to  brake  build  up  time  are calculated.

¶1786.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1787.content: - 3.13.6.2.1.1 The  safe  deceleration,  A_safe(V,d),  is  safety  relevant.  This  means  that  for  the calculation of the safe deceleration, all necessary track and train characteristics shall be taken into account.

¶1788.content: - 3.13.6.2.1.2 The train and track related characteristics to be considered are:

¶1789.content: - The  speed  dependent  deceleration  model(s)  for  the  emergency  brake  either acquired  as  part  of  Train  Data  (see  3.13.2.2.3.1)  or  derived  from  the  brake percentage using the conversion model (see 3.13.3.3)

¶1790.content: - The acceleration/deceleration due to gradient i.e. A_gradient(d) (see 3.13.4)

¶1791.content: -  The locations with reduced adhesion conditions (see 3.13.5)

¶1792.content: - The National Values for reduced adhesion condition (see 3.13.2.3.7.7)

¶1793.content: - The  locations  without  special  brake  contribution  (see  3.13.5),  only  if  the  speed dependent deceleration model(s) for the emergency brake are acquired as part of Train Data

¶1794.content: -  The  rolling  stock  correction  factors  Kdry_rst(V,  EBCL)  and  Kwet_rst(V)  (see 3.13.2.2.9.1), only if the speed dependent deceleration model(s) for the emergency brake are acquired as part of Train Data

¶1795.content: - The National Values for confidence level on emergency brake safe deceleration and for  the  available  wheel/rail  adhesion  (see  3.13.2.3.7.5  &  3.13.2.3.7.6),  only  if  the speed dependent deceleration model(s) for the emergency brake are acquired as part of Train Data

¶1796.content: - The integrated correction factors Kv_int(V) (with the two pivot deceleration values for passenger trains) and Kr_int(l) (see 3.13.2.3.7), only if the conversion model is used

¶1798.content: -  The acquired train length L_TRAIN (see 3.13.2.2.11), only if the conversion model is used

¶1800.content: For  locations  with  normal  adhesion  conditions  and  for  locations  with  reduced adhesion conditions when A_MAXREDADH does not limit to a maximum value the speed dependent deceleration for the emergency brake:

¶1802.content: For locations with reduced adhesion conditions when A_MAXREDADH limits to a maximum value the speed dependent deceleration for the emergency brake:

¶1808.content: If the conversion model is used:

¶1903.content: # 3.13.7 Determination of Most Restrictive Speed Profile (MRSP)

¶1904.content: - 3.13.7.1 The  Most  Restrictive  Speed  Profile  (MRSP)  is  a  description  of  the  most  restrictive speed restrictions the train shall obey on a given piece of track.

¶1905.content: - 3.13.7.2 The Most Restrictive Speed Profile shall be computed from all speed restrictions (see 3.13.2.2.13 & 3.13.2.3.2) by selecting the most restrictive parts of each element, some elements  being  compensated  by  the  train  length  if  requested  by  trackside  (see 3.11.3.1.3 for SSP, 3.11.4.6 for ASP and 3.11.5.3 for TSR).

¶1906.content: - 3.13.7.3 The Most Restrictive Speed Profile shall be recalculated when any of the elements it is built of is changed.

¶1907.content: Figure 39: Most Restrictive Speed Profile selection

¶1908.content: # 3.13.8 Determination of targets and brake deceleration curves

¶1910.content: - 3.13.8.1.1  A  target  is  defined  by  a  target  location  and  a  target  speed,  to  which  the  train  must decelerate before reaching the target location.

¶1911.content: - 3.13.8.1.2  For that purpose, the on-board equipment shall use brake deceleration curves related to  the  supervised  targets,  from  the  deceleration  values  as  specified  in  sections 3.13.6.2.1, 3.13.6.3.1and 3.13.6.4.

¶1912.content: - 3.13.8.1.3  These deceleration values being speed and distance dependent, a brake deceleration curve shall be calculated piecewise, i.e. it shall be composed of interconnected arcs of

¶1913.content: parabola, each one being based on one of the speed/distance dependent deceleration values (see Figure 38).

¶1915.content: - 3.13.8.2.1  The  on-board  shall  continuously  supervise  a  list  of  targets,  which  may  include  the following types of target:

¶1916.content: - the locations corresponding to a speed decrease of the MRSP (if any), which are in advance of the max safe front end of the train

¶1918.content: -  the End of Authority (EOA) and the Supervised Location (SvL)

¶1919.content: - the  location  deduced  from  the  maximum  permitted  distance  to  run  in  Staff Responsible, with a target speed zero

¶1920.content: - 3.13.8.2.1.1 Note: depending on the information received from trackside and the position of the train, the list of supervised targets may be empty.

¶1921.content: - 3.13.8.2.2  The list of supervised targets shall be re-evaluated when any of the elements it is built of is changed (e.g. new MA and/or track description accepted on-board, EOA and/or SvL  temporarily  supervised  at  the  start  location  of  a  mode  profile,  update  of  stored information in specific situations (see sections A.3.4 and 4.10)).

¶1922.content: - 3.13.8.2.3  A target corresponding to a speed decrease of the MRSP shall be removed from the list  of  supervised  targets  when  the  max  safe  front  end  of  the  train  has  passed  the target location.

¶1924.content: - 3.13.8.3.1  If  a  target  belongs  to  the  MRSP  or  is  an  LOA,  the  on-board  shall  calculate  an  EBD curve based on the safe deceleration A_safe(V,d), that crosses the ceiling speed EBI supervision  limit  (see  3.13.9.2)  at  the  target  location,  and  that  extends  up  to  the location where the target speed is reached (EBD foot).

¶1925.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1926.content: Figure 40: Calculation of the EBD curve with regards to MRSP or LOA target

¶1927.content: - 3.13.8.3.2  If  a target is an  SvL, the on-board shall calculate an Emergency Brake Deceleration (EBD) curve based on the safe deceleration A_safe(V,d) and that reaches zero speed at the SvL.

¶1928.content: - 3.13.8.3.3  If a target is the location at the end of the maximum permitted distance to run in Staff Responsible,  the  on-board  shall  calculate  an  Emergency  Brake  Deceleration  (EBD) curve based on the safe deceleration A_safe(V,d) and that reaches zero speed at this staff responsible end location.

¶1929.content: Figure 41: Calculation of the EBD curve with regards to SvL or SR distance

¶1931.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1932.content: - 3.13.8.4.1  If  a  target  is  an  EOA,  the  on-board  shall  calculate  an  Service  Brake  Deceleration (SBD) curve based on the expected deceleration A_expected(V,d) and that reaches zero speed at this EOA location.

¶1933.content: Figure 42: Calculation of the SBD curve with regards to EOA

¶1935.content: - 3.13.8.5.1  The purpose of the guidance curve (GUI) is to provide a comfortable way of braking for the driver, to avoid excessive wear of the brakes and to save traction energy.

¶1936.content: - 3.13.8.5.2  If  the  National  Value  does  not  inhibit  them,  the  on-board  shall  calculate  a  guidance curve (GUI) for each supervised target, based on the normal service brake deceleration A_normal_service(V,d). The foot of a GUI curve (i.e. the location where the GUI speed is equal to the target speed) shall be:

¶1937.content: - the target location, in case of EOA/SvL

¶1938.content: - the location defined in 3.13.9.3.5.9, for others targets

¶1941.content: - 3.13.9.1.1  In this chapter the following supervision limits are defined:

¶1947.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1949.content: - 3.13.9.1.2  The purpose of the emergency brake intervention supervision limit is to assure that the train will remain within the various limits (in distance/speed) imposed by the trackside.

¶1950.content: - 3.13.9.1.3  The  purpose  of  all  other  supervision  limits  is  to  assist  the  driver  in  preventing  an emergency  brake  intervention  by  maintaining  the  speed  of  the  train  within  the appropriate limits.

¶1952.content: - 3.13.9.2.1  The ceiling supervision limits are derived from the MRSP elements, where the speed is constant (refer to 3.13.7) or from the LOA.

¶1953.content: - 3.13.9.2.2  From  an  MRSP  element  or  from  the  LOA,  the  Permitted  speed,  Warning,  Service brake  intervention  and  Emergency  brake  intervention  supervision  limits  are  defined (see Figure 43).

¶1954.content: - 3.13.9.2.3  For dv_ebi, the following formula shall be applied:

¶1957.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1959.content: - 3.13.9.2.4  dV_ebi_min, dV_ebi_max, V_ebi_min and V_ebi_max are defined as fixed values (See Appendix A.3.1)

¶1960.content: - 3.13.9.2.5  For  dV_sbi,  the  same  formula  as  for  dV_ebi  shall  apply,  dV_sbi_min,  dV_sbi_max, V_sbi_min and V_sbi_max being also defined as fixed values (See Appendix A.3.1)

¶1961.content: - 3.13.9.2.6  For  dV_warning,  the  same  formula  as  for  dV_ebi  shall  apply,  dV_warning_min, dV_warning_max,  V_warning_min  and  V_warning_max  being  also  defined  as  fixed values (See Appendix A.3.1)

¶1962.content: - 3.13.9.2.7  For LOA, the same formulas shall apply, by substituting V_MRSP with the LOA speed.

¶1966.content: - 3.13.9.3.1.1 The braking to target  supervision limits  are  derived  from  the  EBD,  SBD  and  GUI curves.

¶1967.content: - 3.13.9.3.1.2 From  an  EBD  curve,  the  Emergency  brake  intervention  (EBI),  Service  brake intervention  (SBI2),  Warning  (W),  Permitted  speed  (P)  and  Indication  (I)  supervision limits, valid for the estimated speed, are defined as follows(see Figure 45):

¶1970.content: - 3.13.9.3.1.3 From  the  SBD  curve,  Service  brake  intervention  (SBI1),  Warning  (W),  Permitted speed  (P)  and  Indication  (I)  supervision  limits,  valid  for  the  estimated  speed,  are defined as follows (see Figure 46):

¶1971.content: - 3.13.9.3.1.4 No  specific  supervision  limit  is  calculated  from  the  GUI  curve:  it  is  only  used  to adjust the Permitted speed (P) supervision limit, which is obtained either from the EBD or the SBD curve.

¶1972.content: Figure 46: Braking to target supervision limits from SBD curve

¶1974.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.1 If  not  inhibited  by  National  Value,  the  ERTMS/ETCS  on-board  equipment  shall compensate  the  inaccuracy  of  the  speed  measurement  by  taking  into  account  the

¶1975.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶1976.content: speed  under  reading  amount  (V_ura)  at  the  moment  when  the  calculation  is  made: V_delta0 = V_ura (see Figure 45).

¶1977.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.2 The time elapsed between the Emergency brake intervention and the full application of the braking effort is reached (EBD) shall be split into two parts:

¶1978.content: - Time during which the traction effort is still present: T_traction

¶1979.content: - Remaining time during which the traction effort is not present: T_berem

¶1980.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.3 The traction time (T_Traction) shall be defined as follows:

¶1981.content: - when the traction cut-off is implemented:

¶1983.content: - when the traction cut-off is not implemented: T_traction = T_traction_cut_off

¶1984.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.4 Note:  When  the  traction  cut-off  is  implemented,  the  traction  cut-off  command  is triggered  when  passing  the  warning  limit.  The  term  (T_warning  +  T_bs2)  in  the equation above takes this into account, assuming that the warning limit is derived from the EBD.

¶1985.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.5 T_bs2 and T_warning are defined in sections 3.13.9.3.3 and 3.13.9.3.4.

¶1986.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.6 The  remaining  time  with  no  traction  (T_berem)  shall  be  equal  to  MAX(T_be  -T_traction ; 0).

¶1987.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.7 Note: T_Traction exceeding T_be is rather a theoretical case, but is nevertheless included to make the specifications complete.

¶1988.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.8 During T_traction, the estimated acceleration (A_est1) shall be the one measured at the moment when the calculation is made, but limited to positive or null values.

¶1989.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.9 If  T_be > T_traction, the estimated acceleration during T_berem (A_est2) shall be the one measured at the moment when the calculation is made, but limited to values between 0 and +0.4m/s 2 .

¶1990.content: - 3.13.9.3.2.10  The  compensated  speed  and  the  distance  travelled  during  the  time  elapsed between the Emergency brake intervention and the full application of the braking effort is reached shall be derived as follows (see Figure 45):

¶1996.content: 3.13.9.3.2.11  Note: The formula avoids that V_bec is lower than V_target.

¶2000.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.1 For the EOA, the on-board shall calculate the location of the SBI supervision limit (SBI1) valid for the estimated speed, assuming that this latter remains constant during the interval T_bs1, until the SBD curve is reached.

¶2002.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.2 For  an  EBD  based  target,  the  on-board  shall  calculate  the  location  of  the  SBI supervision limit (SBI2) valid for the estimated speed, assuming that this latter remains constant  during  the  interval  T_bs2,  until  the  location  of  the  EBI  supervision  limit  is reached.

¶2004.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.3 If the service brake command is available for use and the service brake feedback is not available for use, T_bs1 and T_bs2 shall be equal to T_bs.

¶2005.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.4 If both the service brake command and the service brake feedback are available for use, T_bs1 and T_bs2 shall by default be set to T_bs. When the service brake is used by  the  driver  in  target  speed  monitoring  or  release  speed  monitoring,  they  shall  be reduced and possibly locked to the respective fixed values of 0s and T_bs2_locked, until  the  ceiling  speed  monitoring  is  entered;  they  are  then  reset  to  T_bs  (refer  to detailed algorithm in Appendix A.3.10).

¶2006.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.4.1 In case T_bs < T_bs2_locked then T_bs2 shall be equal to T_bs2_locked.

¶2007.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.5 If the service brake command is not available for use, T_bs1 and T_bs2 shall be set to zero.

¶2008.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.6 Note:  The  values  T_bs1  and  T_bs2  =  0s  are  defined  to  achieve  the  maximum performance when service brake command is not used.

¶2009.content: - 3.13.9.3.3.7 For display purpose only, the SBI1 speed for the estimated train front end, shall be calculated as follows (see Figure 47):

¶2010.content:
$$
V _ { S B I 1 } ( d _ { e s t f r o n t } ) = V _ { S B D } \left ( d _ { e s t f r o n t } + V _ { e s t } \cdot T _ { b s 1 } \right )
$$

¶2011.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶2013.content: Figure 47: Calculation of SBI1 speed displayed to the driver

¶2018.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

This  simplification,  which  avoids  the  need  of  an  iterated  calculation,  is  however acceptable and necessary since the error made tends to zero when the train reaches the SBI2 supervision limit.

¶2023.content: - 3.13.9.3.4.1 The on-board shall calculate the location of the Warning supervision limit valid for the  estimated  speed,  assuming  that  this  latter  remains  constant  during  the  interval T_warning until the location of the SBI1 (for the EOA) or the SBI2 (for an EBD based target) supervision limit is reached.

¶2025.content:
$$
d _ { W } ( V _ { e s t } ) = d _ { S B I 1 } ( V _ { e s t } ) - V _ { e s t } \cdot T _ { w a r m i n g } \text { for the  } E O A
$$

¶2027.content: 3.13.9.3.4.2 T_warning is defined as a fixed value (refer to A.3.1).

¶2029.content: # ERA * UNISIG * EEIG ERTMS USERS GROUP

¶2030.content: - 3.13.9.3.5.1 In case the calculation of the GUI curve is inhibited, the on-board shall calculate the location  of  the  Permitted  speed  supervision  limit  valid  for  the  estimated  speed, assuming that this latter remains constant during the interval T_driver until the location of  the  SBI1  (for  the  EOA) or the SBI2 (for an EBD based target) supervision limit is reached.

¶2034.content: 3.13.9.3.5.2 T_driver is defined as a fixed value (refer to A.3.1).

¶2035.content: - 3.13.9.3.5.3 Note: The reference for the Permitted speed supervision limit is the SBI supervision limit  and  not  the  Warning  supervision  limit.  As  a  result  the  permitted  and  warning supervision limits are clearly separated and do not affect each other. In this way it is clear that the warning is not part of the critical performance interval.

¶2036.content: - 3.13.9.3.5.4 In  case  the  calculation  of  the  Guidance  curve  is  enabled,  the  on-board  shall calculate the location of the Permitted speed supervision limit valid for the estimated speed, as follows:

¶2039.content: - 3.13.9.3.5.5 In case the calculation of the GUI curve is inhibited, for display purpose only, the P speed related to SBD shall be calculated for the estimated train front end as follows:

¶2040.content:
$$
5 \text { speed related to SBD shall be calculated for the estimated traffic} \\ V _ { P } ( d _ { \text {estfront} } ) = V _ { S B D } ( d _ { \text {estfront} } + V _ { \text {est} } \cdot ( T _ { d r i v e } + T _ { b s 1 } ) ) \\ E O A \\ V _ { P } ( d _ { \text {estfront} } ) = 0 \text { if } d _ { \text {estfront} } + V _ { \text {est} } \cdot ( T _ { d r i v e } + T _ { b s 1 } ) \geq d _ { E O A } \\ E O A
$$

¶2041.content: - 3.13.9.3.5.6 In case the calculation of the GUI curve is enabled, for display purpose only, the P speed related to SBD shall be calculated for the estimated train front end as follows:

¶2042.content:
$$
V _ { P } ( d _ { e s t r o f n } ) = \min \left \{ V _ { S B D } ( d _ { e s t r o f n } + V _ { e s t } \cdot ( T _ { d r i v e } + T _ { b s 1 } ) ) , V _ { G U I } ( d _ { e s t r o f n } ) \right \} \\ V _ { P } ( d _ { e s t r o f n } ) = 0 \text { if } d _ { e s t r o f n } + V _ { e s t } \cdot ( T _ { d r i v e } + T _ { b s 1 } ) \geq d _ { E o A } \\
$$

¶2043.content: - 3.13.9.3.5.7 In case the calculation of the GUI curve is inhibited, for display purpose only, the P speed related to EBD, shall be calculated for the max safe front end of the train as follows (see Figure 49):

This  simplification,  which  avoids  the  need  of  an  iterated  calculation,  is however acceptable and necessary since the error made tends to zero when the train reaches the Permitted speed supervision limit.

$$
V _ { P } \left ( d _ { \max s a f e r f o n } \right ) & = \\ & E B D \, \arg e t \\ \max \left \{ \min \left \{ V _ { E B D } \left ( d _ { \max s a f e r f o n } + V _ { e s t } \cdot ( T _ { d r i v e } + T _ { b s 2 } ) + D _ { b e d l a p s y } \right ) - ( V _ { d e l a 0 } + V _ { d e l a 1 } + V _ { d e l a 2 } ) \right \} \right \} , V _ { t r a g e t } \right \} \\ & \left \{ V _ { G U } \left ( d _ { \max s a f e r f o n } \right ) \\ E B D \, \arg e t
$$
